39	## Scope daemon end-to-end integration test coverage
40	
41	- [ ] **Scope daemon end-to-end integration test coverage** (`c-mo1mqlju`)
42	
43	## Separate writs (obligations) from rigs (attempts) — the mult
44	
45	- [ ] **Resolve the seal-engine workspace conflict — session container vs bare clone on host** (`c-mo3ibfq3`)
46	- [ ] **Move rig construction from Spider to the Fabricator** (`c-mo3ibgzt`)
47	
48	## Unlock autonomous hopper-based operation for Nexus
49	
50	- [ ] **Resolve plan-vs-plan conflicts when two Astrolabe sessions overlap** (`c-mo1mqcwz`)
51	- [ ] **Detect and recover anima sessions where the LLM is wedged (e.g. internal Bash 'until' loop on a stale PID) — session.status='running' is no longer a liveness signal** (`c-moevx2i0`)
52	- [ ] **Detect and recover orphaned code-engine attempts — engine.status='running' with no liveness check means a dispatcher crash mid-engine leaves the attempt running forever (sibling problem to the anima-session wedge case)** (`c-moildpau`)
53	- [ ] **Detect and recover anima sessions where the agent ended its turn cleanly (stop_reason=end_turn/stop_sequence, no pending tool_use) but the claude --print process never exits — dangling MCP-proxy SSE connection on 127.0.0.1 keeps node's event loop alive, so babysitter waits forever on close. Observed on rig-moix23cr-7b5e574d (revise engine, ses-moizmsvh-0d927d60): final assistant turn at 19:47Z said 'Work is complete', babysitter and claude child still alive 2h+ later with established TCP pair to MCP proxy port. Heartbeats keep firing so lastActivityAt is misleading — real liveness signal is transcript mtime.** (`c-moj6u6yi`)
54	- [ ] **Auto-recover stuck-after-finish sessions via terminal-stop-reason watchdog. Heuristic: when session.status='running' AND last assistant transcript entry has stop_reason in {end_turn, stop_sequence} AND that entry contains no tool_use blocks AND now - transcript-mtime > IDLE_THRESHOLD (e.g. 5–10 min), the agent has finished but the process won't exit. Recovery: SIGTERM the babysitter pgid — its terminal phase will synthesize a result from the accumulated transcript and the rig advances. Could live as a Clockworks standing order or a Spider checker hook. Pairs with the MCP-shutdown bug click as the auto-recovery layer (treat the symptom mechanically while the root-cause investigation continues). Note real liveness signal is transcript mtime, not session.lastActivityAt — heartbeats lie.** (`c-moj6ue1g`)
55	- [ ] **Fix the 'babysitter.test.ts' hang — but the root cause turns out to be environmental, not a test bug. Investigation update (2026-04-29 cdd4c6ee): the unit tests themselves are fine. Running 'node --test --test-timeout=30000 src/**/*.test.ts' from a normal shell, all 17 babysitter tests pass in 4 seconds. The '--test-timeout=0' we kept seeing in 'ps' output (along with --inspect-publish-uid=stderr,http --inspect-port=127.0.0.1:9229) is being injected by VS Code's Node.js auto-attach mechanism — the daemon runs in a VS Code-monitored terminal (pts/3 in dev container) and VS Code auto-attach extends the spawned node child's argv with debugger/inspector flags including --test-timeout=0. Anima session babysitters spawned from the daemon inherit the same auto-attach environment, so EVERY 'pnpm test' invocation under any agent session loses its test timeout — mechanical-check steps, animas writing-then-running tests, etc. all fall victim when something legitimately misbehaves. So the 'fix' is closer to environment hygiene than test surgery: (1) make the daemon and its descendants opt out of auto-attach (run with NODE_OPTIONS=--require=... that strips inspector flags, or set 'debug.javascript.autoAttachFilter': 'disabled' in the dev container, or wrap the daemon launch with 'env -u VSCODE_INSPECTOR_OPTIONS' or similar); (2) optionally also set an explicit '--test-timeout=<N>' in package.json test scripts as belt-and-suspenders so even if auto-attach injects 0, the explicit flag takes precedence (last-wins on the node CLI). Originally observed wedging engines on rig-moj12h4o-19af85f4 (review's mechanical-test step) and a reviewer session in 56c811c2; six orphan node --test processes accumulated across sessions and were reaped manually. Related: c-moizriyk (reviewer-wedge symptom), c-moj6u6yi (MCP-shutdown bug, separate symptom), c-moj6ue1g (terminal-stop-reason watchdog, recovery layer).** (`c-moj7h6r7`)
56	- [ ] **Investigate recurring 'database is locked' errors in daemon stderr. Patterns observed across multiple sessions: '[animator] Failed to update guild_alive_at: database is locked', '[animator] Failed to record transcript for ses-...: database is locked', '[animator] Back-off observer failed for ses-...: database is locked', '[daemon] crawl() error: database is locked'. SQLite WAL mode + busy_timeout should serialize concurrent writers cleanly; persistent 'locked' errors suggest either (a) some write paths bypass the busy-handler retry (raw exec without retry wrapper), (b) a transaction holds a write lock while awaiting an async operation that also needs to write (deadlock-by-async, where the second waiter inside the same JS event loop can never make progress because the first is blocked on its own re-entry), or (c) a long-running read transaction blocking the WAL checkpoint. Investigation steps: enumerate all sessions.put/transcripts.put/state.put call sites and check for missing busy-timeout wrappers; look for any code path where a transaction is opened, then awaits an external promise that also touches the same DB; check WAL file size on disk to see if checkpoints are succeeding. The error doesn't appear fatal — operations are retried implicitly on the next tick — but it represents real work loss (transcript writes the animator gives up on, heartbeats that miss, Spider crawl ticks that abort) and the noise hides genuine failures in the log.** (`c-mojdtg62`)
57	- [ ] **Make the planner embed canonical content from the brief into the mandate, rather than referencing it by pointer** (`c-mo21apns`)
58	- [ ] **Make the implementer block (fail loud) when its inputs reference content it cannot access, rather than fabricating plausible substitutes** (`c-mo21aqo6`)
59	- [ ] **Extract testable deliverables from brief/spec — produce a structured walkthrough list of what the patron should be able to do after the commission lands** (`c-mo3dj7v7`)
60	- [ ] **Ship MVP Overseer: nsg overseer commission — promote one draft writ from the new pool to open** (`c-moaz1q9b`)
61	- [ ] **Add named Clockworks events for petition transitions — emit petition.accepted, petition.deferred, petition.declined, petition.stalled (and possibly petition.fresh) as named Clockworks events alongside the existing auto-wired book.reckoner.reckonings.created CDC channel. Rejected in the Reckonings book design (Open Question 5) on grounds that auto-wired CDC + in-relay outcome filter serves every Clockworks consumer; revisit when (a) a non-Clockworks consumer of the petition stream appears (external webhook, dashboard service) that benefits from a flat named-event payload, (b) measurable performance penalty appears in the in-relay filter path, or (c) the Clockworks signal validator's reserved-namespace policy gains teeth on book.* events and the same pass extends teeth to petition.*. Implementation note: the named-events emitter would naturally hang off the same CDC handler as the staleness snapshot updater (sibling click c-moixpj1l) — both are derived signals over the Reckonings stream, so colocating keeps the cross-cutting concerns in one place.** (`c-moj6mjep`)

15	  under the new routing they'd infinite-loop. But it expands scope from "rename the mapping" to "delete three rigs + one engine + one config
16	   field." If you'd rather scope this tighter (just the rename + mapping), the two older rigs stay but become unreachable dead code, which
17	  contradicts the codified "prefer removal" default. My read is the deletion is correct; flagging in case your read differs.
18	  3. Non-terminal brief writs in production — there are 3 right now (2 new, 1 open) per the vibers database. I left migration as an operator
19	   concern (cancel+repost, or direct patch). Let me know if you want a shim.

21	  'brief' to 'mandate'. I wrote this as no special coordination needed, but that does mean a brief window where the observation-lift brief
22	  is wrong. Acceptable?
23	  5. Complexity rating — feels like a 5 to me. Mostly mechanical (deletions), but across multiple files and with the mapping-collision

35	  The verification subtree is 7 live children deep into what "rig-level automated verification" should look like. A lot of those questions —
36	   what belongs in automated verification beyond tests pass?, where does verification sit in the pipeline?, does it subsume the reviewer? —
37	  are shaped by what kinds of mistakes the system actually makes when left alone. We don't have that data yet because we haven't run
38	  unattended.
39	
40	  Designing verification now means designing against imagined failure modes. We'd either under-engineer (miss real patterns) or
41	  over-engineer (build scaffolding for hypothetical problems). Stage 1 completion is what produces the empirical ground Stage 2 needs.
42	  There's a reason the ladder is ordered the way it is — it's not arbitrary.
43	
44	  Notifications is small and narrowly scoped
45	
46	  No decomposed children yet. Probably one design session resolves: what events trigger a notification, what channel, what's in the payload,
47	   what's the patron's response surface. Compare to verification, which is a multi-week design arc even after you've decided what to tackle
48	  first.
49	
50	  The reviewer prereq
51	
52	  Verification explicitly has a child asking about its relationship to the existing reviewer engine (c-mo3djcvz) and the X013 quality
53	  scorer. But the diagnostic click "Why does the reviewer rarely reject?" (c-mo1mqbnr) is still live under Stage 2. You probably want an
54	  answer to that before designing something that may subsume or extend it — otherwise you risk designing verification as a
55	  reviewer-replacement when the actual problem might be a reviewer-calibration issue.
56	
57	  Operational argument
58	
59	  Right now: retry primitive shipped, so recoverable failures heal themselves. But if a commission goes stuck non-retryable, or a rig drifts
60	   in some way the retry cap can't help with, you find out by… checking. That's the last thing between "post and walk away" and being
61	  actually able to walk away. Notifications converts the hopper from "reliably works unattended if you remember to check" to "reliably pings
62	   you when you need to look."
63	
64	  Recommendation
65	
66	  Notifications first. Verification can — and probably should — wait until we've accumulated some real unattended-operation data to ground
67	  the design in. Want to open a design conversation on notifications?

Drafted at .scratch/brief-remove-brief-writ-type.md.

  What this commission touches

  The removal cascades: brief type → plan-and-ship mapping changes → two-phase-planning and three-phase-planning become unreachable →
  spec-publish (their only-consumer engine) has no callers → generatedWritType (its only-consumer config) becomes dead code. Pulling the
  thread pulls the whole disused path out.

  Things worth your eye

  1. Spider/Astrolabe mapping collision — both kits want to map mandate and the current registration logic rejects duplicates. I left the
  mechanism open to the implementer with one constraint: deterministic winner. Three reasonable resolutions (Astrolabe priority,
  guild-config override convention, or Spider drops its default). Do you want to pin one, or leave it open?
  2. Cascade decision: retiring the two older rigs — I treated this as non-negotiable because (a) the combined rig supersedes them and (b)
  under the new routing they'd infinite-loop. But it expands scope from "rename the mapping" to "delete three rigs + one engine + one config
   field." If you'd rather scope this tighter (just the rename + mapping), the two older rigs stay but become unreachable dead code, which
  contradicts the codified "prefer removal" default. My read is the deletion is correct; flagging in case your read differs.
  3. Non-terminal brief writs in production — there are 3 right now (2 new, 1 open) per the vibers database. I left migration as an operator
   concern (cancel+repost, or direct patch). Let me know if you want a shim.
  4. Migration/coordination with observation-lift — both commissions land independently; whichever lands second updates its hardcoded
  'brief' to 'mandate'. I wrote this as no special coordination needed, but that does mean a brief window where the observation-lift brief
  is wrong. Acceptable?
  5. Complexity rating — feels like a 5 to me. Mostly mechanical (deletions), but across multiple files and with the mapping-collision
  wrinkle that requires a real design call.

  Read when you have time — happy to iterate.

  ----------------------


  I'd pick c-mo1z3teo (notifications), and I'd argue it's not close. Here's why:

  The empirical-grounding argument

  The verification subtree is 7 live children deep into what "rig-level automated verification" should look like. A lot of those questions —
   what belongs in automated verification beyond tests pass?, where does verification sit in the pipeline?, does it subsume the reviewer? —
  are shaped by what kinds of mistakes the system actually makes when left alone. We don't have that data yet because we haven't run
  unattended.

  Designing verification now means designing against imagined failure modes. We'd either under-engineer (miss real patterns) or
  over-engineer (build scaffolding for hypothetical problems). Stage 1 completion is what produces the empirical ground Stage 2 needs.
  There's a reason the ladder is ordered the way it is — it's not arbitrary.

  Notifications is small and narrowly scoped

  No decomposed children yet. Probably one design session resolves: what events trigger a notification, what channel, what's in the payload,
   what's the patron's response surface. Compare to verification, which is a multi-week design arc even after you've decided what to tackle
  first.

  The reviewer prereq

  Verification explicitly has a child asking about its relationship to the existing reviewer engine (c-mo3djcvz) and the X013 quality
  scorer. But the diagnostic click "Why does the reviewer rarely reject?" (c-mo1mqbnr) is still live under Stage 2. You probably want an
  answer to that before designing something that may subsume or extend it — otherwise you risk designing verification as a
  reviewer-replacement when the actual problem might be a reviewer-calibration issue.

  Operational argument

  Right now: retry primitive shipped, so recoverable failures heal themselves. But if a commission goes stuck non-retryable, or a rig drifts
   in some way the retry cap can't help with, you find out by… checking. That's the last thing between "post and walk away" and being
  actually able to walk away. Notifications converts the hopper from "reliably works unattended if you remember to check" to "reliably pings
   you when you need to look."

  Recommendation

  Notifications first. Verification can — and probably should — wait until we've accumulated some real unattended-operation data to ground
  the design in. Want to open a design conversation on notifications?

Earlier commit message in `1508818` was wrong about this; corrected here with raw V8 profile evidence. Switched analyzer to DA records. Workflow guide updated to remove the false claim and add a "Note on the lcov import baseline" explaining the 62-of-124-lines-hit-on-import phenomenon that originally tripped me up. |

28	| `cedc5f1` | `spider.testCommand` and `spider.variables.testCommand` changed `pnpm test` → `pnpm coverage` so coverage threshold failures flow into the spider review engine's mechanical-check step → reviewer prompt → revise engine. |

31	
32	| Commit | What |
33	|---|---|
34	| `112b9589` | coco-log: framework coverage scaffolding baseline. |
35	| `b3b0a3ea` | coco-log: coverage threshold gate + tail-bias truncation. |

42	
43	```sh
44	# 1. Pick a package
45	pnpm test:uniqueness <pkg>            # e.g. framework/core, plugins/spider, plugins/clerk
46	                                      # accepts shortforms — see docs/guides/trimming-tests.md
47	# Optional: pnpm test:uniqueness <pkg> --workers 4 --filter <regex>

50	cat coverage/uniqueness/<pkg-flat-name>.md
51	
52	# 3. Spot-check redundancy candidates → decide keep/delete using the
53	#    assertion peek column. Watch for parameter sweeps that share
54	#    line coverage but assert distinct input→output mappings.

56	# 4. Edit test files to delete decided candidates. No auto-delete.

59	cd packages/<pkg> && pnpm test:coverage   # tests still pass
60	cd /workspace/nexus && pnpm coverage      # aggregate floor still holds (exit 0)
61	
62	# 6. Re-run analyzer to confirm

65	# 7. Commit per package, update the per-package status table at

67	```
68	
69	If `pnpm coverage` exits 1, you over-trimmed; restore some tests
70	and try again. The aggregate gate is the safety net.
71	
72	## Recommended order of attack
73	
74	(Also documented in `docs/guides/trimming-tests.md`.)
75	
76	1. **`framework/core`** — 39 tests / 311 test lines. Probed: 29
77	   pure-redundant (74%). Smallest real package; validates the
78	   workflow on a real trim.
79	2. **`framework/arbor`** — 113 tests / 1,833 test lines. Probed:
80	   90 pure-redundant (80%). Small monolith, two files.
81	3. **`plugins/stacks`** — already 97.7% line coverage. Likely few
82	   wins; do it for completeness.
83	4. Mid-size plugins (`tools`, `codexes`, `copilot`, `lattice`,
84	   `lattice-discord`, `fabricator`, `loom`).
85	5. Larger plugins (`oculus`, `parlour`, `cartograph`, `claude-code`,
86	   `sentinel`, `astrolabe`, `clockworks`, `reckoner`).
87	6. **The headline targets:** `clerk` (7,148 test lines), `clockworks`
88	   if not yet done, **`spider`** (18,714 test lines — the headline
89	   prize). Spider is ~6× larger than the next biggest, with
90	   modest 70.7% line coverage; expected biggest absolute win.
91	
92	## What to capture for X017
93	
94	Each trim session is a data point for **`X017 — Test Redundancy in
95	Agent-Written Code`** (spec at
96	`/workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X017-test-redundancy/spec.md`).
97	
98	Per package, append a YAML row to
99	`/workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X017-test-redundancy/artifacts/<YYYY-MM-DD>-<pkg>.yaml`
100	with:
101	
102	- pre/post counts (test files, test lines, total tests, pure-redundant)
103	- pre/post coverage (line / branch / function from `pnpm coverage`)
104	- candidates reviewed / deleted / kept
105	- a sampling of "kept" tests with reason categories (parameter sweep /
106	  edge case / error path / representative / other)
107	- wall-clock duration
108	- commit SHA
109	
110	The spec lists five hypotheses (H1–H5); the YAML rows are the data
111	that lets you eventually evaluate them.
112	
113	If you'd rather not collect X017 data on a given session — totally
114	fine. The trim still works as straight engineering. The experiment is
115	opportunistic; data points are valuable but not required for trim
116	itself.
117	
118	## Things I almost got wrong (so you don't)
119	
120	These are the gotchas I burned hours on. They're documented in code
121	comments and the workflow guide, but flagging here too:
122	
123	1. **`--test-name-pattern` separator is a SINGLE SPACE.** Not `' > '`,
124	   not `/`, not `>`. node:test joins suite titles + test name with one
125	   space. Wrong separator silently matches zero tests but **still
126	   reports `count=1`** because the file itself is wrapped as a
127	   passing meta-test. So you can't tell from the test count alone
128	   whether the pattern actually worked. Always check FNDA/BRDA values
129	   in the lcov to verify a real test ran.
130	
131	2. **The lcov import baseline is real, not a bug.** When you run a
132	   test in isolation, ~half the lines of any imported source file
133	   show hit=1 in the lcov *before* the test body runs. Those are
134	   genuinely covered at module-load time (top-level imports,
135	   constants, function declarations). It's NOT "every line marked
136	   hit=1 regardless of execution" — that's what I initially thought,
137	   and I was wrong. Verify hit-count distribution with
138	   `awk -F',' '{print $2}' | sort | uniq -c`.
139	
140	3. **Tests with name collisions get skipped.** If two tests in a file
141	   share the same `<suite> <name>` joined string, `--test-name-pattern`
142	   can't uniquely select either. The analyzer warns and excludes
143	   them rather than misattribute. Real but rare.
144	
145	## Open questions / further work
146	
147	- **Branch-coverage signal.** Current analyzer uses line coverage. The
148	  guide notes that two tests covering the same line but taking
149	  different branches will both appear redundant under this signal.
150	  The aggregate gate's branch floor (80%) catches over-trimming, but
151	  if you want a finer signal, the FNDA+BRDA implementation is in git
152	  at `1508818`. Keep DA for now (consistent with the gate, easier to
153	  inspect); revisit if data suggests otherwise.
154	
155	- **Per-test wall-clock is ~2× the package's `pnpm test` time** on
156	  first run, ~0.5s on cached re-runs. For `spider` (762 tests) expect
157	  roughly 4–5 minutes fresh. Worth letting it run while you do other
158	  things.
159	
160	- **Greedy reduction is non-optimal.** Picks one valid redundant set,
161	  not the maximum. If we ever want tighter trim, run with `--filter`
162	  scopes (file-by-file) and compare. Out of scope for the first pass.
163	
164	- **Empty describe blocks left after deletion.** The analyzer flags
165	  but doesn't auto-clean. If you delete every test in a describe, the
166	  describe shell stays behind. Worth a sweep at the end of each
167	  package's trim.
168	
169	## Quick-start commands
170	
171	```sh
172	# Get oriented
173	cat /workspace/nexus/docs/guides/trimming-tests.md
174	
175	# Read the spec for X017 (if you want to instrument)
176	cat /workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X017-test-redundancy/spec.md
177	
178	# Start a trim
179	cd /workspace/nexus
180	pnpm test:uniqueness framework/core
181	cat coverage/uniqueness/framework-core.md
182	```
183	
184	## Files I'd read next session, in order
185	
186	1. This file (you are here).
187	2. `/workspace/nexus/docs/guides/trimming-tests.md` — the workflow.
188	3. `/workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X017-test-redundancy/spec.md` —
189	   the experiment frame.
190	4. `/workspace/nexus/scripts/test-uniqueness.ts` — only if you need
191	   to debug something or extend the analyzer. Otherwise the doc is
192	   enough.
193	
194	That's it. Have fun cutting.

1	# Handoff — Distiller pipeline cost optimization
2	
3	The wrap-up's distiller pipeline currently costs **~$3.30/cycle**
4	($2.27 distill + $1.04 verify on Sonnet 4.5). Sean flagged this as
5	surprising during the previous session's wrap-up; the
6	investigation surfaced both *why* it costs that much and a clear
7	two-step optimization that should bring it down ~6–10x.
8	
9	This handoff is the work-line for `c-monrru42` — the distiller
10	optimization. Tight, ~1-hour shaped, **no API cost to develop**
11	(can dry-run against existing transcripts at
12	`experiments/data/transcripts/*.jsonl`).
13	
14	## Where we are
15	
16	- Click `c-monrru42` is live, captures the optimization spec.
17	- Pipeline source:
18	  - Driver: `bin/coco-distill.sh`
19	  - Distiller agent: `.claude/agents/distiller.md` (Sonnet)
20	  - Verifier agent: `.claude/agents/verifier.md` (Sonnet)
21	- No work has started on the optimization itself — only the
22	  cost analysis.
23	
24	## The cost analysis
25	
26	Per-wrap-up token usage (deduped by `message.id` from the
27	distiller's session JSONL):
28	
29	| | Distill | Verify |
30	|---|---:|---:|
31	| Unique API calls | 42 | 21 |
32	| Cache create | 281k | 140k |
33	| Cache read | **3.5M** | **1.2M** |
34	| Output | 11.5k | 10.2k |
35	| Peak single-call context | 167k | 140k |
36	| **Cost (Sonnet 4.5)** | **~$2.27** | **~$1.04** |
37	
38	**Why the cache-read is so high.** The distiller reads the 1.5MB
39	/ ~120k-token transcript across ~42 chunked-Read turns
40	(transcripts exceed `Read`'s default 2000-line limit, so the
41	agent reads in segments). Each subsequent turn carries the
42	accumulated context forward. Cache-read sums to ~3.5M tokens
43	(roughly: 42 turns × avg ~85k context = 3.5M).
44	
45	**Source-of-truth references for the cost analysis:**
46	
47	- The previous session's distiller log:
48	  `~/.claude/projects/-workspace-nexus-mk2/838bbb55-1b9a-4920-aee7-0f8990feedb4.jsonl`
49	- The verifier log:
50	  `~/.claude/projects/-workspace-nexus-mk2/1ba07783-0a53-4e1b-b418-34a9c227ea0e.jsonl`
51	- The corresponding distill output:
52	  `docs/planning/2026-05-02-lab-daemon-fixture-phase-2a.md`
53	
54	If the optimization changes either pipeline, **re-run the
55	analysis** against a fresh wrap-up's JSONL to confirm the
56	reduction is real.
57	
58	## The two-step optimization
59	
60	### Step 1 — Pre-filter the transcript (the bigger win)
61	
62	Most of the 1.5MB transcript is bash-tool noise: ls dumps, file
63	contents, daemon log tails, JSON outputs, jsonlines. The
64	distiller doesn't need any of it — it needs the human-readable
65	chat substance (assistant text + user messages).
66	
67	A jq pre-filter step in `bin/coco-distill.sh` should drop everything
68	that isn't `assistant.content[].text` or `user.content` (string
69	form), and write the filtered transcript to a temp file. The
70	distiller then reads *that* file. Estimated input reduction:
71	**5–10x** (bash output is the bulk).
72	
73	Sketch:
74	
75	```sh
76	FILTERED="$(mktemp --suffix=.jsonl)"
77	trap 'rm -f "$FILTERED"' EXIT
78	
79	jq -c 'select(
80	  (.message.role == "user" and (.message.content | type) == "string") or
81	  (.message.role == "assistant" and (.message.content | type) == "array"
82	    and (.message.content | any(.type == "text")))
83	) | {
84	  role: .message.role,
85	  text: (.message.content | if type == "string" then . else map(select(.type == "text") | .text) | join("\n") end)
86	}' "$TRANSCRIPT" > "$FILTERED"
87	```
88	
89	Worth experimenting with what to keep vs. drop:
90	
91	- Definitely keep: assistant text blocks, user messages.
92	- Definitely drop: tool_use blocks (the call args), tool_result
93	  blocks (the output).
94	- **Edge case to think about:** `thinking` blocks. They're often
95	  load-bearing for understanding what the agent decided. The
96	  distiller may benefit from seeing them. Test both ways.
97	- **Edge case:** the user's slash-command outputs (e.g.
98	  `/context`, `/wrap-up`) appear as user messages with structured
99	  content. Keep those — they often carry meaningful instructions
100	  + context.
101	
102	### Step 2 — Switch to a one-shot prompt
103	
104	Once the transcript is small enough to fit in a single context,
105	the chunked-Read pattern becomes pointless. Pass the filtered
106	transcript inline in the initial prompt:
107	
108	```sh
109	DISTILL="$(claude --add-dir "$SANCTUM" --agent distiller \
110	    -p "$(cat <<EOF
111	Distill this transcript:
112	
113	\`\`\`jsonl
114	$(cat "$FILTERED")
115	\`\`\`
116	EOF
117	)" --output-format text)"
118	```
119	
120	The distiller agent's prompt also needs to change — drop the
121	"read the file at $TRANSCRIPT" instructions, replace with "the
122	transcript is inline above; produce the distill in one assistant
123	turn."
124	
125	After both steps: distill should drop from ~$2.27 → ~$0.20–0.50.
126	
127	### Optional step 3 — Haiku for the verifier
128	
129	The verifier's task ("does the distill match the transcript?") is
130	mechanical compared to distillation. Worth trying Haiku 3.5 (or
131	whatever the current cheap model is) — that's another ~10x cost
132	reduction on the verify side (~$1.04 → ~$0.10).
133	
134	Verifier agent file already specifies `model: sonnet`. Change to
135	`model: haiku` and re-run the analysis. If verification quality
136	holds (verifier is calibrated to stay silent on green; failure
137	mode would be surfacing false-positive findings or missing real
138	ones), keep the change.
139	
140	## How to validate
141	
142	Pick an existing transcript with known-good distill output —
143	ideally `9a768036-061d-404d-b306-b3a72c49d5f4` (this session) or
144	`927ab84f-c92d-4a65-94ac-a19daf97ee42` (the previous lab session).
145	Both have existing distills in `docs/planning/` for reference.
146	
147	For each iteration of the optimization:
148	
149	1. Run the modified pipeline against the test transcript.
150	2. Diff the new distill against the existing one. Substantive
151	   differences (missing decisions, missing notable moments, drift
152	   in research observations) = quality regression.
153	3. Sum the new pipeline's tokens from the JSONL.
154	4. Compare cost: target is roughly **$0.20–0.40 / cycle** end-to-
155	   end after both steps; **$0.10–0.20** with Haiku verifier.
156	
157	If the filtered-transcript approach loses something important
158	(e.g. the distiller relies on tool_use to know what files were
159	read), iterate on what to keep in the filter.
160	
161	## Risks / edge cases
162	
163	- **The filtered transcript loses tool-call context.** A
164	  distiller that wants to say "Coco read the X016 spec" can't
165	  see the tool_use that fetched it. This is probably fine — the
166	  distill cares about decisions and outcomes, not method. But
167	  worth checking the existing distills for places where
168	  tool-call awareness mattered.
169	- **Some sessions are mostly tool-use, little chat.** A
170	  refactoring session might be 95% tool calls with sparse chat.
171	  Filtering that down might leave too little signal. Consider
172	  keeping a *summary* of tool-use rather than full content —
173	  e.g. `[Read /workspace/nexus/...]` as a compressed marker.
174	  Probably out of scope for the first pass.
175	- **Multi-MB transcripts that exceed model context even after
176	  filtering.** Unlikely in practice — even very long sessions
177	  rarely break 100k tokens of pure chat. Worth checking the
178	  filtered size for the longest existing transcript before
179	  committing.
180	
181	## Pointers
182	
183	- **Click**: `c-monrru42`
184	- **Cost-analysis script** (the Python deduping over JSONL — handy
185	  for the validation step): inline in the previous session's
186	  transcript around the first cost discussion. Easy to recreate.
187	- **Existing transcripts for testing**:
188	  `experiments/data/transcripts/*.jsonl` — sessions of varying
189	  shape and length.
190	- **Existing distills** (for diffing):
191	  `docs/planning/2026-05-02-*.md` and earlier dates.
192	
193	## Relationship to other clicks
194	
195	This is independent of the Laboratory work line (`c-monew2rg`,
196	`c-monewa82`, `c-momm4abc`, `c-momm4i15`). Either work-line can be
197	picked up first; they don't block each other. Distiller-cost is
198	the better choice when there's no API budget for the session.
199

# Handoff — Distiller pipeline cost optimization

The wrap-up's distiller pipeline currently costs **~$3.30/cycle**
($2.27 distill + $1.04 verify on Sonnet 4.5). Sean flagged this as
surprising during the previous session's wrap-up; the
investigation surfaced both *why* it costs that much and a clear
two-step optimization that should bring it down ~6–10x.

This handoff is the work-line for `c-monrru42` — the distiller
optimization. Tight, ~1-hour shaped, **no API cost to develop**
(can dry-run against existing transcripts at
`experiments/data/transcripts/*.jsonl`).

## Where we are

- Click `c-monrru42` is live, captures the optimization spec.
- Pipeline source:
  - Driver: `bin/coco-distill.sh`
  - Distiller agent: `.claude/agents/distiller.md` (Sonnet)
  - Verifier agent: `.claude/agents/verifier.md` (Sonnet)
- No work has started on the optimization itself — only the
  cost analysis.

## The cost analysis

Per-wrap-up token usage (deduped by `message.id` from the
distiller's session JSONL):

| | Distill | Verify |
|---|---:|---:|
| Unique API calls | 42 | 21 |
| Cache create | 281k | 140k |
| Cache read | **3.5M** | **1.2M** |
| Output | 11.5k | 10.2k |
| Peak single-call context | 167k | 140k |
| **Cost (Sonnet 4.5)** | **~$2.27** | **~$1.04** |

**Why the cache-read is so high.** The distiller reads the 1.5MB
/ ~120k-token transcript across ~42 chunked-Read turns
(transcripts exceed `Read`'s default 2000-line limit, so the
agent reads in segments). Each subsequent turn carries the
accumulated context forward. Cache-read sums to ~3.5M tokens
(roughly: 42 turns × avg ~85k context = 3.5M).

**Source-of-truth references for the cost analysis:**

- The previous session's distiller log:
  `~/.claude/projects/-workspace-nexus-mk2/838bbb55-1b9a-4920-aee7-0f8990feedb4.jsonl`
- The verifier log:
  `~/.claude/projects/-workspace-nexus-mk2/1ba07783-0a53-4e1b-b418-34a9c227ea0e.jsonl`
- The corresponding distill output:
  `docs/planning/2026-05-02-lab-daemon-fixture-phase-2a.md`

If the optimization changes either pipeline, **re-run the
analysis** against a fresh wrap-up's JSONL to confirm the
reduction is real.

## The two-step optimization

### Step 1 — Pre-filter the transcript (the bigger win)

Most of the 1.5MB transcript is bash-tool noise: ls dumps, file
contents, daemon log tails, JSON outputs, jsonlines. The
distiller doesn't need any of it — it needs the human-readable
chat substance (assistant text + user messages).

A jq pre-filter step in `bin/coco-distill.sh` should drop everything
that isn't `assistant.content[].text` or `user.content` (string
form), and write the filtered transcript to a temp file. The
distiller then reads *that* file. Estimated input reduction:
**5–10x** (bash output is the bulk).

Sketch:

```sh
FILTERED="$(mktemp --suffix=.jsonl)"
trap 'rm -f "$FILTERED"' EXIT

jq -c 'select(
  (.message.role == "user" and (.message.content | type) == "string") or
  (.message.role == "assistant" and (.message.content | type) == "array"
    and (.message.content | any(.type == "text")))
) | {
  role: .message.role,
  text: (.message.content | if type == "string" then . else map(select(.type == "text") | .text) | join("\n") end)
}' "$TRANSCRIPT" > "$FILTERED"
```

Worth experimenting with what to keep vs. drop:

- Definitely keep: assistant text blocks, user messages.
- Definitely drop: tool_use blocks (the call args), tool_result
  blocks (the output).
- **Edge case to think about:** `thinking` blocks. They're often
  load-bearing for understanding what the agent decided. The
  distiller may benefit from seeing them. Test both ways.
- **Edge case:** the user's slash-command outputs (e.g.
  `/context`, `/wrap-up`) appear as user messages with structured
  content. Keep those — they often carry meaningful instructions
  + context.

### Step 2 — Switch to a one-shot prompt

Once the transcript is small enough to fit in a single context,
the chunked-Read pattern becomes pointless. Pass the filtered
transcript inline in the initial prompt:

```sh
DISTILL="$(claude --add-dir "$SANCTUM" --agent distiller \
    -p "$(cat <<EOF
Distill this transcript:

\`\`\`jsonl
$(cat "$FILTERED")
\`\`\`
EOF
)" --output-format text)"
```

The distiller agent's prompt also needs to change — drop the
"read the file at $TRANSCRIPT" instructions, replace with "the
transcript is inline above; produce the distill in one assistant
turn."

After both steps: distill should drop from ~$2.27 → ~$0.20–0.50.

### Optional step 3 — Haiku for the verifier

The verifier's task ("does the distill match the transcript?") is
mechanical compared to distillation. Worth trying Haiku 3.5 (or
whatever the current cheap model is) — that's another ~10x cost
reduction on the verify side (~$1.04 → ~$0.10).

Verifier agent file already specifies `model: sonnet`. Change to
`model: haiku` and re-run the analysis. If verification quality
holds (verifier is calibrated to stay silent on green; failure
mode would be surfacing false-positive findings or missing real
ones), keep the change.

## How to validate

Pick an existing transcript with known-good distill output —
ideally `9a768036-061d-404d-b306-b3a72c49d5f4` (this session) or
`927ab84f-c92d-4a65-94ac-a19daf97ee42` (the previous lab session).
Both have existing distills in `docs/planning/` for reference.

For each iteration of the optimization:

1. Run the modified pipeline against the test transcript.
2. Diff the new distill against the existing one. Substantive
   differences (missing decisions, missing notable moments, drift
   in research observations) = quality regression.
3. Sum the new pipeline's tokens from the JSONL.
4. Compare cost: target is roughly **$0.20–0.40 / cycle** end-to-
   end after both steps; **$0.10–0.20** with Haiku verifier.

If the filtered-transcript approach loses something important
(e.g. the distiller relies on tool_use to know what files were
read), iterate on what to keep in the filter.

## Risks / edge cases

- **The filtered transcript loses tool-call context.** A
  distiller that wants to say "Coco read the X016 spec" can't
  see the tool_use that fetched it. This is probably fine — the
  distill cares about decisions and outcomes, not method. But
  worth checking the existing distills for places where
  tool-call awareness mattered.
- **Some sessions are mostly tool-use, little chat.** A
  refactoring session might be 95% tool calls with sparse chat.
  Filtering that down might leave too little signal. Consider
  keeping a *summary* of tool-use rather than full content —
  e.g. `[Read /workspace/nexus/...]` as a compressed marker.
  Probably out of scope for the first pass.
- **Multi-MB transcripts that exceed model context even after
  filtering.** Unlikely in practice — even very long sessions
  rarely break 100k tokens of pure chat. Worth checking the
  filtered size for the longest existing transcript before
  committing.

## Pointers

- **Click**: `c-monrru42`
- **Cost-analysis script** (the Python deduping over JSONL — handy
  for the validation step): inline in the previous session's
  transcript around the first cost discussion. Easy to recreate.
- **Existing transcripts for testing**:
  `experiments/data/transcripts/*.jsonl` — sessions of varying
  shape and length.
- **Existing distills** (for diffing):
  `docs/planning/2026-05-02-*.md` and earlier dates.

## Relationship to other clicks

This is independent of the Laboratory work line (`c-monew2rg`,
`c-monewa82`, `c-momm4abc`, `c-momm4i15`). Either work-line can be
picked up first; they don't block each other. Distiller-cost is
the better choice when there's no API budget for the session.

1	# Handoff — Laboratory phase 2 + small follow-ups
2	
3	The Laboratory MVP is **done and producing real data.** First
4	real-world trial (X016 phase 1) ran end-to-end against vibers,
5	extracted artifacts live at
6	`experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/artifacts/2026-05-01-baseline-validation/`.
7	
8	This handoff covers what to pick up next: one big piece (X016 phase 2
9	— daemon lifecycle), two small follow-ups, and the open grit clicks.
10	The full apparatus + canonical spec (`packages/laboratory/README.md`)
11	is the reference for everything else.
12	
13	## Where we are
14	
15	```
16	0834fd0f X016 trial 1: first successful Laboratory apparatus run
17	8d9396cf laboratory: rename probe-trial-context fields for scope clarity   ← latest
18	```
19	
20	210/210 unit tests passing; typecheck clean; codified pipeline smoke
21	test green.
22	
23	## Phase 1 — what landed in this thread
24	
25	- Three standard probes (`lab.probe-{trial-context, stacks-dump, git-range}`)
26	- Archive engine (`lab.archive`) + `lab-trial-archives` book + tightened teardown safety check
27	- Two new probe books (`lab-trial-stacks-dumps`, `lab-trial-codex-commits`)
28	- Three retrieval CLI tools (`lab-trial-show`, `lab-trial-extract`, `lab-trial-export-book`)
29	- Codified pipeline smoke test (synthetic-engine integration test)
30	- Stable-pin enforcement at manifest load — file/link/range/dist-tag/branch refs rejected
31	- Per-trial framework-version pin via npx bootstrap (Option F + H)
32	- X016 spec, brief, manifest authored
33	- First real-world trial run, extracted, archived
34	- Architecture doc, README authoring guide, retired predecessor spec
35	
36	Two real bugs caught + fixed by the first trial:
37	
38	- `lab.probe-stacks-dump` used CommonJS `require('node:fs')` in an ESM
39	  module → replaced with proper imports.
40	- `lab.archive`'s graft didn't pass `${writ}` through; `buildArchiveGraft`
41	  now emits `writ: '${writ}'` alongside the trial-context injection.
42	  (Spider's `resolveGivens` does substitute it for graft givens — same
43	  mechanism the static template uses for pre-graft engines.)
44	
45	One field rename for clarity:
46	
47	- `lab.probe-trial-context` summary fields renamed:
48	  `frameworkVersion` → `labHostFrameworkVersion`,
49	  `pluginsInstalled` → `labHostPluginsInstalled`. The trial-pinned
50	  framework version is in `manifestSnapshot.frameworkVersion`.
51	
52	## What's next — main work
53	
54	### X016 phase 2 — daemon lifecycle in lab.guild-setup (`c-monew2rg`)
55	
56	The blocker for measuring actual orientation behavior. Phase 1
57	posted commissions with `waitForTerminal: false` because the test
58	guild has no Spider crawl loop running. Phase 2 changes that.
59	
60	Sean's confirmation from the conversation: install `spider` into
61	the test guild and run `nsg crawl-continual` to drive the rig.
62	
63	**Likely shape:**
64	
65	1. Add `spider` (and probably `clockworks`) to the test guild's
66	   plugin pin list. Both are published @ `0.1.292` so this just
67	   means extending the manifest's `plugins[]` array.
68	
69	2. After fixture init in `lab.guild-setup`, spawn a long-running
70	   `nsg --guild-root <X> crawl-continual` (or `nsg start`) as a
71	   detached child process. Capture its pid in a yield so teardown
72	   can stop it.
73	
74	3. `lab.guild-teardown` SIGTERMs the captured pid before rm-rf'ing
75	   the guild dir. (Lab.guild-teardown already runs *after* archive
76	   succeeds, so the rig has already finished by then.)
77	
78	4. `lab.commission-post-xguild` switches to `waitForTerminal: true`.
79	   The test guild's spider drives the rig forward; the engine polls
80	   `nsg writ show` until `classification === 'terminal'`.
81	
82	**Open design questions** (worth thinking through before
83	implementation):
84	
85	- **Wallclock cap.** `waitForTerminal`'s `timeoutMs` (default 30m)
86	  caps how long the scenario engine waits, but the test guild's
87	  daemon could keep running past that. Should `lab.guild-teardown`
88	  forcibly stop the daemon on every terminal-state, regardless of
89	  whether the rig completed cleanly? Probably yes — orphaning a
90	  child process across rig boundaries is exactly the "failed-trial
91	  orphan" pattern the existing `c-momm4abc` click tracks.
92	- **Backgrounding mechanics.** `child_process.spawn` with
93	  `detached: true` + `unref()` + redirected stdio so the parent (the
94	  laboratory engine) can exit while the child keeps running. Pidfile
95	  in the test guild's `.nexus/clock.pid`-equivalent spot for
96	  teardown lookup.
97	- **`nsg start` vs `nsg crawl-continual`.** `nsg start` boots the
98	  full daemon (clockworks, oculus HTTP server, spider crawl loop).
99	  `nsg crawl-continual` is just the spider loop. For a trial, we
100	  may not need oculus or HTTP servers — `crawl-continual` is leaner.
101	  But standing orders that fire on `writ.ready` etc. are clockworks-
102	  driven; if the trial's workload depends on those, we need the
103	  full daemon. Probably start with `nsg start` for compatibility,
104	  optimize to `crawl-continual` later if it matters.
105	- **Crash recovery.** If the lab-host crashes mid-trial, the
106	  detached test-guild daemon keeps running. On lab-host restart we
107	  have no way to find it. Option: write the pid into the trial
108	  writ's `ext.laboratory.runtime` slot at setup; a recovery scan on
109	  lab-host startup can SIGTERM lingering daemons of completed/
110	  failed trials. Defer to a follow-up if it bites.
111	
112	**Acceptance**: posting an X016 phase 2 trial (waitForTerminal=true,
113	brief = baseline-task.md) results in a real implementer session
114	record in the test guild's `animator/sessions` book, captured by
115	`lab.probe-stacks-dump`, with non-zero turn count + cost. Then a
116	strong-prompt variant runs as a sibling trial; analysis compares.
117	
118	### Small follow-ups
119	
120	**`c-monewa82` — `rigTemplate: null` in probe summary.** The
121	trial-context probe captures `rigTemplate: null` even though the rig
122	was instantiated from `laboratory.post-and-collect-default`. The
123	probe reads `spider.show(rigId)` and looks for `rigDoc.templateId`
124	or `rigDoc.template` defensively. Need to investigate whether
125	Spider's `RigDoc` actually carries the template name under some
126	other field, or whether it doesn't store it post-spawn. If the
127	latter, derive from `Spider.listTemplates()` + writ-type matching.
128	Small bug; doesn't affect any current functionality.
129	
130	**`c-momm4abc` — Failed-trial orphan cleanup.** When setup engines
131	succeed but a downstream engine fails, teardowns get cancelled
132	(rather than running unconditionally). This left orphan codex+guild
133	fixtures during the X016 trial-1 debugging cycle. Two design
134	directions: setup engines grab a release-on-rig-terminal handle, or
135	teardowns run unconditionally on rig failure. Both have implications
136	for the rig template's engine wiring.
137	
138	**`c-momm4i15` — Daemon ↔ CLI Scriptorium state drift.** `nsg codex
139	remove` from the CLI updates `guild.json` but the running daemon's
140	in-memory Scriptorium isn't notified; the daemon's next write
141	reverts orphan removals. Cosmetic. Vibers currently has three
142	`verify-fixtures-*` orphans in its codex registry that survive
143	daemon restarts because the CLI removal doesn't stick.
144	
145	## Active clicks at handoff
146	
147	```
148	c-moma9llq  Implementation — turn the Laboratory MVP0 design into a working apparatus  [LIVE]
149	├── c-monew2rg   X016 phase 2 — daemon lifecycle (this is the main next piece)         [LIVE]
150	├── c-monewa82   rigTemplate null in probe summary                                      [LIVE]
151	├── c-momm4abc   Failed-trial orphan cleanup                                            [LIVE]
152	├── c-momm4i15   Daemon vs CLI Scriptorium state drift                                  [LIVE]
153	└── (every other child concluded)
154	
155	c-momaab8y   Port the first real-world trial                                            [CONCLUDED]
156	```
157	
158	The implementation parent (`c-moma9llq`) is technically still LIVE
159	because of the four follow-ups; could conclude it as "MVP done,
160	follow-ups tracked individually" if we want a cleaner tree.
161	
162	## Pointers
163	
164	- **Canonical spec**: `packages/laboratory/README.md` —
165	  architecture, book schemas, atomicity, CLI surface, plugin-pin
166	  rules, bootstrap, authoring guide.
167	- **Architecture doc**: `/workspace/nexus/docs/architecture/apparatus/laboratory.md`.
168	- **First trial artifacts**:
169	  `experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/artifacts/2026-05-01-baseline-validation/`.
170	- **First trial spec**: `experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/spec.md`.
171	- **Manifest example with stable pins + frameworkVersion**:
172	  `experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/manifests/baseline-apparatus-validation.yaml`.
173	
174	## Live environment state
175	
176	- Vibers' daemon is running (pid 1783777, started at the bottom of
177	  this thread). Three known cosmetic orphan codexes (`verify-fixtures-*`)
178	  in its registry; harmless but won't clear without daemon restart.
179	- No orphan fixtures on disk in vibers' `.nexus/laboratory/`
180	  (codex/guild dirs are clean).
181	- All commits land on `main` in both `nexus-mk2` and `nexus`. The
182	  `nexus` repo has two commits ahead of origin/main but nothing has
183	  been pushed (per the no-push-without-explicit-ask directive).
184	
185	## Tone note
186	
187	X016 phase 2 is the next big piece, but it's not gated on anything
188	specific — pick it up when next you have a chunk of time. The
189	follow-up clicks are nice-to-haves; the apparatus works without
190	fixing any of them.

# Handoff — Laboratory phase 2 + small follow-ups

The Laboratory MVP is **done and producing real data.** First
real-world trial (X016 phase 1) ran end-to-end against vibers,
extracted artifacts live at
`experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/artifacts/2026-05-01-baseline-validation/`.

This handoff covers what to pick up next: one big piece (X016 phase 2
— daemon lifecycle), two small follow-ups, and the open grit clicks.
The full apparatus + canonical spec (`packages/laboratory/README.md`)
is the reference for everything else.

## Where we are

```
0834fd0f X016 trial 1: first successful Laboratory apparatus run
8d9396cf laboratory: rename probe-trial-context fields for scope clarity   ← latest
```

210/210 unit tests passing; typecheck clean; codified pipeline smoke
test green.

## Phase 1 — what landed in this thread

- Three standard probes (`lab.probe-{trial-context, stacks-dump, git-range}`)
- Archive engine (`lab.archive`) + `lab-trial-archives` book + tightened teardown safety check
- Two new probe books (`lab-trial-stacks-dumps`, `lab-trial-codex-commits`)
- Three retrieval CLI tools (`lab-trial-show`, `lab-trial-extract`, `lab-trial-export-book`)
- Codified pipeline smoke test (synthetic-engine integration test)
- Stable-pin enforcement at manifest load — file/link/range/dist-tag/branch refs rejected
- Per-trial framework-version pin via npx bootstrap (Option F + H)
- X016 spec, brief, manifest authored
- First real-world trial run, extracted, archived
- Architecture doc, README authoring guide, retired predecessor spec

Two real bugs caught + fixed by the first trial:

- `lab.probe-stacks-dump` used CommonJS `require('node:fs')` in an ESM
  module → replaced with proper imports.
- `lab.archive`'s graft didn't pass `${writ}` through; `buildArchiveGraft`
  now emits `writ: '${writ}'` alongside the trial-context injection.
  (Spider's `resolveGivens` does substitute it for graft givens — same
  mechanism the static template uses for pre-graft engines.)

One field rename for clarity:

- `lab.probe-trial-context` summary fields renamed:
  `frameworkVersion` → `labHostFrameworkVersion`,
  `pluginsInstalled` → `labHostPluginsInstalled`. The trial-pinned
  framework version is in `manifestSnapshot.frameworkVersion`.

## What's next — main work

### X016 phase 2 — daemon lifecycle in lab.guild-setup (`c-monew2rg`)

The blocker for measuring actual orientation behavior. Phase 1
posted commissions with `waitForTerminal: false` because the test
guild has no Spider crawl loop running. Phase 2 changes that.

Sean's confirmation from the conversation: install `spider` into
the test guild and run `nsg crawl-continual` to drive the rig.

**Likely shape:**

1. Add `spider` (and probably `clockworks`) to the test guild's
   plugin pin list. Both are published @ `0.1.292` so this just
   means extending the manifest's `plugins[]` array.

2. After fixture init in `lab.guild-setup`, spawn a long-running
   `nsg --guild-root <X> crawl-continual` (or `nsg start`) as a
   detached child process. Capture its pid in a yield so teardown
   can stop it.

3. `lab.guild-teardown` SIGTERMs the captured pid before rm-rf'ing
   the guild dir. (Lab.guild-teardown already runs *after* archive
   succeeds, so the rig has already finished by then.)

4. `lab.commission-post-xguild` switches to `waitForTerminal: true`.
   The test guild's spider drives the rig forward; the engine polls
   `nsg writ show` until `classification === 'terminal'`.

**Open design questions** (worth thinking through before
implementation):

- **Wallclock cap.** `waitForTerminal`'s `timeoutMs` (default 30m)
  caps how long the scenario engine waits, but the test guild's
  daemon could keep running past that. Should `lab.guild-teardown`
  forcibly stop the daemon on every terminal-state, regardless of
  whether the rig completed cleanly? Probably yes — orphaning a
  child process across rig boundaries is exactly the "failed-trial
  orphan" pattern the existing `c-momm4abc` click tracks.
- **Backgrounding mechanics.** `child_process.spawn` with
  `detached: true` + `unref()` + redirected stdio so the parent (the
  laboratory engine) can exit while the child keeps running. Pidfile
  in the test guild's `.nexus/clock.pid`-equivalent spot for
  teardown lookup.
- **`nsg start` vs `nsg crawl-continual`.** `nsg start` boots the
  full daemon (clockworks, oculus HTTP server, spider crawl loop).
  `nsg crawl-continual` is just the spider loop. For a trial, we
  may not need oculus or HTTP servers — `crawl-continual` is leaner.
  But standing orders that fire on `writ.ready` etc. are clockworks-
  driven; if the trial's workload depends on those, we need the
  full daemon. Probably start with `nsg start` for compatibility,
  optimize to `crawl-continual` later if it matters.
- **Crash recovery.** If the lab-host crashes mid-trial, the
  detached test-guild daemon keeps running. On lab-host restart we
  have no way to find it. Option: write the pid into the trial
  writ's `ext.laboratory.runtime` slot at setup; a recovery scan on
  lab-host startup can SIGTERM lingering daemons of completed/
  failed trials. Defer to a follow-up if it bites.

**Acceptance**: posting an X016 phase 2 trial (waitForTerminal=true,
brief = baseline-task.md) results in a real implementer session
record in the test guild's `animator/sessions` book, captured by
`lab.probe-stacks-dump`, with non-zero turn count + cost. Then a
strong-prompt variant runs as a sibling trial; analysis compares.

### Small follow-ups

**`c-monewa82` — `rigTemplate: null` in probe summary.** The
trial-context probe captures `rigTemplate: null` even though the rig
was instantiated from `laboratory.post-and-collect-default`. The
probe reads `spider.show(rigId)` and looks for `rigDoc.templateId`
or `rigDoc.template` defensively. Need to investigate whether
Spider's `RigDoc` actually carries the template name under some
other field, or whether it doesn't store it post-spawn. If the
latter, derive from `Spider.listTemplates()` + writ-type matching.
Small bug; doesn't affect any current functionality.

**`c-momm4abc` — Failed-trial orphan cleanup.** When setup engines
succeed but a downstream engine fails, teardowns get cancelled
(rather than running unconditionally). This left orphan codex+guild
fixtures during the X016 trial-1 debugging cycle. Two design
directions: setup engines grab a release-on-rig-terminal handle, or
teardowns run unconditionally on rig failure. Both have implications
for the rig template's engine wiring.

**`c-momm4i15` — Daemon ↔ CLI Scriptorium state drift.** `nsg codex
remove` from the CLI updates `guild.json` but the running daemon's
in-memory Scriptorium isn't notified; the daemon's next write
reverts orphan removals. Cosmetic. Vibers currently has three
`verify-fixtures-*` orphans in its codex registry that survive
daemon restarts because the CLI removal doesn't stick.

## Active clicks at handoff

```
c-moma9llq  Implementation — turn the Laboratory MVP0 design into a working apparatus  [LIVE]
├── c-monew2rg   X016 phase 2 — daemon lifecycle (this is the main next piece)         [LIVE]
├── c-monewa82   rigTemplate null in probe summary                                      [LIVE]
├── c-momm4abc   Failed-trial orphan cleanup                                            [LIVE]
├── c-momm4i15   Daemon vs CLI Scriptorium state drift                                  [LIVE]
└── (every other child concluded)

c-momaab8y   Port the first real-world trial                                            [CONCLUDED]
```

The implementation parent (`c-moma9llq`) is technically still LIVE
because of the four follow-ups; could conclude it as "MVP done,
follow-ups tracked individually" if we want a cleaner tree.

## Pointers

- **Canonical spec**: `packages/laboratory/README.md` —
  architecture, book schemas, atomicity, CLI surface, plugin-pin
  rules, bootstrap, authoring guide.
- **Architecture doc**: `/workspace/nexus/docs/architecture/apparatus/laboratory.md`.
- **First trial artifacts**:
  `experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/artifacts/2026-05-01-baseline-validation/`.
- **First trial spec**: `experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/spec.md`.
- **Manifest example with stable pins + frameworkVersion**:
  `experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/manifests/baseline-apparatus-validation.yaml`.

## Live environment state

- Vibers' daemon is running (pid 1783777, started at the bottom of
  this thread). Three known cosmetic orphan codexes (`verify-fixtures-*`)
  in its registry; harmless but won't clear without daemon restart.
- No orphan fixtures on disk in vibers' `.nexus/laboratory/`
  (codex/guild dirs are clean).
- All commits land on `main` in both `nexus-mk2` and `nexus`. The
  `nexus` repo has two commits ahead of origin/main but nothing has
  been pushed (per the no-push-without-explicit-ask directive).

## Tone note

X016 phase 2 is the next big piece, but it's not gated on anything
specific — pick it up when next you have a chunk of time. The
follow-up clicks are nice-to-haves; the apparatus works without
fixing any of them.

1	# Handoff — Laboratory phase 2b + small follow-ups
2	
3	The daemon-fixture engines are **shipped and validated against a real
4	trial.** Phase 2a smoke-test ran 16/16 engines green end-to-end on
5	2026-05-02; daemon comes up, idles, comes down clean.
6	
7	This handoff covers what's next: **phase 2b** (real implementer
8	execution + first `animator/sessions` capture), the three small
9	follow-ups still parked, and the lingering vibers operational
10	issues.
11	
12	## Where we are
13	
14	```
15	ba548127 session-notes(lab-impl-2): wrap-up                                    ← latest
16	e3cff686 X016 phase 2a — first daemon-fixture trial completed end-to-end
17	1f5f820e laboratory: lab.daemon-setup/teardown fixture pair for phase-2 trials
18	12545ee5 session-notes(lab-implementation): wrap-up
19	```
20	
21	228/228 unit tests passing; typecheck clean; build clean. Main is
22	pushed to origin.
23	
24	## Phase 2a — what landed in the previous session
25	
26	**Engines (`packages/laboratory/src/engines/daemon-fixture.ts`):**
27	
28	- `lab.daemon-setup` — auto-allocates ephemeral 127.0.0.1 ports for
29	  tool HTTP server + Oculus, deep-merges into test guild's
30	  `guild.json`, refuses if a stale `.nexus/daemon.pid` exists, shells
31	  out `<localNsg> --guild-root <X> start`, reads pidfile, yields
32	  `{ guildPath, daemonPid, toolServerPort, oculusPort, pidFile,
33	  logsDir }`. `nsg start`'s built-in detach + 10-s startup-sync
34	  carries the heavy lifting; the engine is a thin wrapper.
35	- `lab.daemon-teardown` — archive-presence safety check, then shells
36	  out `<localNsg> stop` (idempotent). Tolerant of missing local-nsg
37	  binstub. Does NOT delete files — guild-teardown owns rm-rf.
38	
39	**Shape decision: separate fixture pair, not steps inside guild-setup.**
40	Three reasons codified in the engine docstring + README: (1) opt-in
41	matches reality (phase-1 trials don't want a daemon), (2) composes
42	with future patterns (multi-daemon, restart-mid-trial), (3) matches
43	existing fixture decomposition.
44	
45	**18 new tests** covering port allocation, port-config deep-merge,
46	test-guild discovery, validation, pidfile pre-flight guard,
47	archive-row safety check, missing-binstub tolerance.
48	
49	**README updated** with a `Driving rigs with a test-guild daemon`
50	section showing the four-fixture wiring pattern.
51	
52	**Phase 2a smoke trial:**
53	
54	- Trial: `w-monlfejq-2e343b788e0d`, rig `rig-monlffd3-d32f317e`,
55	  archive `lar-monlfqir-a25da3488822`
56	- Wallclock: ~16s (npx bootstrap + 7 plugin installs + daemon
57	  up/down)
58	- Codex: `/workspace/nexus` at local HEAD `3c307a20a7af` (per Sean
59	  — trial codex is the framework repo, NOT nexus-mk2)
60	- Plugin set: stacks/tools/codexes/clerk + spider/clockworks/
61	  fabricator. No animator/loom/claude-code/astrolabe yet.
62	- `waitForTerminal: false` because no mandate→rig mapping is
63	  configured, so Spider has nothing to dispatch and idle-crawls.
64	- Auto-port-allocation worked: tools=40833, oculus=32901, no
65	  collision with vibers' 7471/7470.
66	- Reverse-topo teardown order ran exactly as designed: daemon
67	  → guild → codex.
68	- Extracted artifacts:
69	  `experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/artifacts/2026-05-02-baseline-2a-daemon-smoke/`
70	
71	`c-momm4abc` orphan-cleanup click amended to flag daemon pidfiles
72	as part of the eventual orphan-recovery sweep.
73	
74	## What's next — main work
75	
76	### Phase 2b — first implementer-driven trial (`c-monew2rg`)
77	
78	The phase-2a smoke test validated the daemon plumbing. Phase 2b
79	flips on `waitForTerminal: true` and gets the test guild to
80	**actually execute** a rig — capturing the first real
81	`animator/sessions` row from a trial. Cost: ~$0.30 of API per trial.
82	
83	Sean's directives from the previous conversation:
84	
85	- Trial codex is **`/workspace/nexus`**, not `/workspace/nexus-mk2`.
86	  Brief shape needs to match the framework repo (the existing
87	  `briefs/baseline-task.md` says "Function lives in
88	  `src/util/numeric.ts`" — needs targeting at a specific package).
89	- All standard implementer plumbing is fine to install (animator,
90	  loom, claude-code-apparatus). Vibers does this every day.
91	- API costs are accepted.
92	
93	**Open design questions for phase 2b** (worth a thinking pass at
94	the top of the next session):
95	
96	1. **Plugin set + rig template.** Two paths:
97	   - **(a) Install astrolabe.** Get the kit-side `mandate →
98	     astrolabe.plan-and-ship` mapping for free. Heavyweight rig (13
99	     engines: planner phase + analyst + patron-anima + reviewer +
100	     implementer + seal). Pollutes pure-implementer-turn-count
101	     measurement with planner overhead.
102	   - **(b) Skip astrolabe, configure `mandate → default` via
103	     `spider.rigTemplateMappings` in guild.json.** Spider ships the
104	     `default` template (`draft → implement → review → revise →
105	     seal`) but doesn't auto-register the mapping. Lighter
106	     measurement surface.
107	   - **My lean: (b).** The whole point of X016 is measuring
108	     orientation tax in the implementer specifically. Planner
109	     overhead is noise.
110	2. **Role definition.** The implementer summons via
111	   `animator.summon({ role, prompt, cwd })`. Vibers uses
112	   `artificer` with `clerk:* tools:*` permissions. The test guild
113	   needs a comparable role — either via `givens.config` deep-merge
114	   into guild.json's `loom.roles`, or by copying vibers'
115	   `loom.roles.artificer` block, or by authoring a slim role
116	   purpose-built for X016 trials.
117	3. **Brief tuning.** The current `baseline-task.md` says "create
118	   `src/util/numeric.ts`" + "tests pass under `pnpm test`." For the
119	   nexus monorepo, top-level `src/util/numeric.ts` won't be picked
120	   up by any package's test command. Either retarget to a specific
121	   package (e.g. `packages/framework/cli/src/util/numeric.ts` with
122	   the corresponding test command) or pick a different small task
123	   that fits the monorepo shape cleanly.
124	4. **`spider.variables`.** Default rig template references
125	   `${vars.role}`, `${vars.buildCommand}`, `${vars.testCommand}`.
126	   These have to be set in the test guild's `guild.json` under
127	   `spider.variables`. Mirror vibers' values:
128	   `role=implementer`/`role=artificer`,
129	   `buildCommand="pnpm build"`, `testCommand="pnpm test"` —
130	   adjusted per the targeted package/path.
131	5. **Timeout.** `waitForTerminal`'s `timeoutMs` default is 30
132	   minutes. A baseline orientation trial probably runs in <10
133	   minutes; pin it explicitly so a runaway session doesn't burn
134	   budget.
135	6. **Anthropic API access.** The test guild's claude-code session
136	   needs ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. Vibers inherits this from the
137	   environment; the test guild's `nsg start` will inherit too as
138	   long as we don't strip it. Verify the daemon-setup engine
139	   passes env through (it should — `execFile` doesn't strip env by
140	   default).
141	
142	**Acceptance:** phase-2b trial completes with a real
143	`animator/sessions` row in the test guild's stacks dump showing
144	non-zero turn count + non-zero cost; codex commit count > 0; the
145	implementer either succeeds (writ → completed) or fails predictably
146	on the brief (writ → failed with a specific reason). Either
147	outcome is a successful phase-2b validation — what matters is that
148	the real implementer flow ran and we captured data on it.
149	
150	After phase 2b lands, **phase 2c** is the actual experiment:
151	baseline + strong-prompt variants, multiple runs each, comparative
152	analysis sanctum-side via `nsg lab trial-export-book` + DuckDB.
153	Phase 2c is its own design pass — strong-prompt brief authoring,
154	N-runs strategy, sample-size logic.
155	
156	### Small follow-ups (still parked)
157	
158	**`c-monewa82` — `rigTemplate: null` in probe summary.** Confirmed
159	still real in the phase-2a trial extract (line 16 of the trial-
160	context yaml: `rigTemplate: null`). The probe reads
161	`spider.show(rigId)` and looks for `rigDoc.templateId` /
162	`rigDoc.template` defensively. Investigate whether Spider's
163	`RigDoc` exposes the template name under a different field, or
164	derive from `Spider.listTemplates()` + writ-type matching. Tight,
165	~30-min shaped.
166	
167	**`c-momm4abc` — Failed-trial orphan cleanup.** Updated last
168	session to note that daemon pidfiles + orphan daemon processes are
169	part of the orphan inventory. Two design directions: (1) setup
170	engines grab a release-on-rig-terminal handle, (2) teardowns run
171	unconditionally on rig failure. Cosmetic; failed-trial dev loops
172	accumulate cruft but nothing breaks.
173	
174	**`c-momm4i15` — Daemon ↔ CLI Scriptorium drift.** `nsg codex
175	remove` doesn't notify the running daemon; daemon's next write
176	reverts the orphan removal. Vibers currently has three
177	`verify-fixtures-*` orphan codexes that survive daemon restarts
178	because of this. Cosmetic but visibly noisy in `daemon.err` (one
179	clone-failure entry per orphan per background-fetch tick).
180	
181	## Vibers operational issues observed during phase 2a
182	
183	**Pre-existing, not phase-2 caused, but worth a janitor pass at
184	some point:**
185	
186	1. **Stuck old rig, cleared.** `rig-mobylvo0-566d3e71` (on mandate
187	   `w-moby5pum-10ec54220ba7`, "collapse brief into mandate" from
188	   2026-04-23) was wedging Spider's `animator-paused` block-
189	   checker with a Zod fault — every crawl tick threw, blocking
190	   ALL dispatch on vibers. Cancelled to unblock the phase-2a
191	   trial. Likely worth opening a click for "Spider continues
192	   crawling when a single block-checker throws" — currently one
193	   bad rig stalls everything.
194	2. **Recurring DB-locked errors.** Vibers' daemon log shows
195	   chronic `[animator] Failed to record session ses-modnht6d-
196	   eabc1b73: database is locked` and `[daemon] crawl() error:
197	   database is locked`. The ses-modnht6d session is some old zombie
198	   the animator keeps retrying. Vibers' DB is 1.7GB and only the
199	   daemon process holds it; lock contention is probably internal
200	   thread contention on a hot path. Transient enough that the
201	   phase-2a trial completed despite it. Worth a click for
202	   "vibers DB hygiene" eventually.
203	3. **Three orphan codexes.** Per `c-momm4i15` — `verify-fixtures-
204	   {1267530e, 2244ce82, 40dd2663}` keep failing background clones
205	   because the bare repos don't exist. Sean removed them via CLI
206	   weeks ago but the daemon's in-memory state never got the memo.
207	   Cosmetic spam; no functional impact.
208	
209	## Pointers
210	
211	- **Engine source**: `packages/laboratory/src/engines/daemon-fixture.ts`
212	- **Engine tests**: `packages/laboratory/src/engines/daemon-fixture.test.ts`
213	- **Phase-2a manifest**: `experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/manifests/baseline-execution-2a.yaml`
214	- **Phase-2a artifacts**: `experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/artifacts/2026-05-02-baseline-2a-daemon-smoke/`
215	- **Canonical spec**: `packages/laboratory/README.md` —
216	  architecture, book schemas, atomicity, CLI surface, plugin-pin
217	  rules, bootstrap, daemon fixture, authoring guide.
218	- **X016 spec**: `experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/spec.md`
219	- **Default rig template** (the candidate for phase 2b's
220	  `mandate → default` mapping):
221	  `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/spider/src/default-template.ts`
222	- **Vibers' production loom config** (reference for phase-2b role
223	  config): `/workspace/vibers/guild.json` lines 28–46.
224	
225	## Active clicks at handoff
226	
227	```
228	c-moma9llq  Implementation — Laboratory MVP0 working apparatus              [CONCLUDED]
229	c-monew2rg  X016 phase 2 — daemon lifecycle + full implementer execution     [LIVE]
230	            (engines built + 2a validated; 2b/2c remaining)
231	c-monewa82  rigTemplate null in probe summary                                 [LIVE]
232	c-momm4abc  Failed-trial orphan cleanup (+ daemon pidfiles)                   [LIVE]
233	c-momm4i15  Daemon vs CLI Scriptorium state drift                             [LIVE]
234	```
235	
236	`c-monew2rg`'s goal text covers the broader phase-2 scope (daemon
237	lifecycle through full implementer execution). Engine code is done;
238	the click stays live until phase 2b validates the implementer path.
239	
240	## Live environment state
241	
242	- **Vibers daemon**: running (pid 1861807, started fresh in the
243	  previous session). Standard ports 7470/7471. Three known cosmetic
244	  orphan codexes still in registry; chronic DB-lock noise still
245	  happening but transient.
246	- **No orphan fixtures on disk**: vibers'
247	  `.nexus/laboratory/{codexes,guilds}/` are clean after phase-2a
248	  teardown.
249	- **All commits land on `main`** in `nexus-mk2`. The `nexus` repo
250	  has 2 commits ahead of origin/main; nothing pushed (per the
251	  no-push-without-explicit-ask directive).
252	- **Authentication**: vibers has a working ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in its
253	  environment (otherwise no animator sessions would work).
254	  Phase-2b test guild will inherit this through `nsg start`.
255	
256	## Tone note
257	
258	Phase 2b is the natural next big piece, but **it is not gated on
259	anything specific** — pick it up when next you have a chunk of time
260	+ budget for ~$0.30 in API calls (and likely 1–2 debug rounds
261	adding another $0.30-0.60). The follow-ups are nice-to-haves; the
262	apparatus works without fixing any of them.

265	a tight palette-cleanser — the bug's still real, ~30-min-shaped, no

pushed to origin.

- `lab.daemon-teardown` — archive-presence safety check, then shells

Three reasons codified in the engine docstring + README: (1) opt-in

existing fixture decomposition.
