content.content:
/workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/data/coco-log.yaml
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 88a35e7d-5e4d-4685-96a1-68607fc9c7f8
  date: 2026-05-02
  item: "Per-test coverage uniqueness analyzer + trim workflow guide (shardworks/nexus@1508818). scripts/test-uniqueness.ts — for any package, spawns one node process per test with --test-name-pattern filtering and --experimental-test-coverage to attribute coverage to individual tests, then greedy-reduces the suite to identify 'pure-redundant' tests whose coverage is fully covered by other tests. Default 4 workers (cap at 8). mtime-keyed cache at <pkg>/coverage/per-test/ for fast iteration (framework/arbor: 8s fresh, 0.5s cached for 113 tests). Outputs both .md (human-readable, includes peek of first assert per test) and .json (full matrix). Three significant gotchas surfaced during build: (a) node:test's --test-name-pattern joins suite titles + test name with a SINGLE SPACE not '/' or ' > '; wrong separator silently matches zero tests while reporting count=1 (the file-as-meta). (b) line coverage (DA records) cannot be used for per-test attribution — V8 marks every reachable line in a loaded module as hit=1 regardless of whether a test exercised it; only FNDA (functions called) and BRDA (branches taken) reflect actual execution, so the matrix uses those as 'coverage units'. (c) tests whose 'suite + name' joined string collides with another test's in the same file are skipped (cannot be uniquely selected). docs/guides/trimming-tests.md — workflow guide explaining methodology, step-by-step trim process, per-package status table for cross-session continuity, and recommended order of attack (smallest first, then monoliths; spider expected to be the headline win at 18.7k test lines / 70.7% line coverage). Initial findings: framework/core (39 tests, 74% redundant), framework/arbor (113 tests, 52% redundant) — meaningful trim opportunity across the monorepo."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: aa69d06c-95c7-4ae9-b0a1-a4747c269ba4
  date: 2026-05-02
  item: "Reverted the Tier-1 distillation pipeline (commits 2230e366 + 93e75a27). Two issues surfaced after shipping: (1) cross-session canonical state was lost — distills are session-scoped, but the click tree is what carried 'what's open / decided' across sessions, and we drifted into treating distills as if they substituted for that; (2) the audience for distill files was unclear — too long for end-of-session verification, redundant with click tree for future-Coco orientation, redundant with transcripts for research narrative. Sean called it: 'I don't even know who the audience for the distill files is anymore.' Removed .claude/agents/distiller.md, .claude/agents/verifier.md, bin/coco-distill.sh, bin/coco-extract-conversation.py; restored coco.md and wrap-up/SKILL.md to pre-pipeline state. Wrote docs/archive/design-sessions/distillation-pipeline-design/00-arc-postmortem.md capturing the full arc — what was tried, what worked conceptually (substrate vs artifact frame, JSONL preprocessor, subagent-as-CLI pattern, verifier severity-bar discipline, hybrid distill format with in-flight inquiries), what didn't (no clear audience, distill-as-click-substitute drift, oversize verification surface), and what to actually try next time. Existing docs/planning/ distills from concurrent sessions left in place as historical artifacts. Back to clicks-only for now."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 88a35e7d-5e4d-4685-96a1-68607fc9c7f8
  date: 2026-05-02
  item: "Methodology correction + X017 spec + handoff doc. Sean pushed back on my 'line coverage is unreliable' claim from the previous commit's message; I dug in with NODE_V8_COVERAGE raw profiles and confirmed I was wrong — DA records ARE accurate per-test, V8 reports counts correctly, the no-match-baseline confusion came from misreading the lcov: 62 of 124 lines hit=1 are LEGITIMATELY covered at module-load time (top-level imports, function declarations, comments), the other 62 hit=0 lines are correctly un-executed function bodies. shardworks/nexus@1122e8b switches the analyzer back to DA records (line coverage), restores naming, and rewrites the workflow guide's methodology section to remove the false claim and add a 'Note on the lcov import baseline' to prevent future readers from making the same mistake. Drafted experiments/X017-test-redundancy/spec.md — research question on agent-written test redundancy with 5 hypotheses (volume, survival, pattern clustering, aggregate-floor-holds, volume↔redundancy correlation). Index moved X017 into Draft section. Wrote .scratch/handoff-test-trim.md as session-closing handoff: TL;DR pointer, recap of all 4 nexus + 1 vibers + 3 sanctum commits, how-to-trim quick-start, recommended order of attack (smallest first, spider as headline target), X017 data-collection guidance, three gotchas-I-burned-hours-on, open questions. Probe data so far: framework/core 39 tests / 74% redundant, framework/arbor 113 tests / 80% redundant under DA-based analysis (line coverage)."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

content.content:
1	# Coco Log — Standing Research Instrument
2	#
3	# One entry per unit of work handled directly by Coco (the interactive
4	# collaborator) rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission.
5	# Parallel to the commission log — together they capture the full
6	# picture of how work is routed in the system.
7	#
8	# An entry is created for each commit (or tight cluster of commits
9	# toward a single goal) that Coco produces during a session.
10	#
11	# Fields:
12	#   session        — Coco's Claude session ID (join key to archived transcripts)
13	#   date           — date the work was done (YYYY-MM-DD)
14	#   item           — short description of the work (one line)
15	#   commissionable — whether this work could have been an autonomous commission

file.content:
# Coco Log — Standing Research Instrument
#
# One entry per unit of work handled directly by Coco (the interactive
# collaborator) rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission.
# Parallel to the commission log — together they capture the full
# picture of how work is routed in the system.

Done. Quick report:

## What landed (commit `bcc4a1c7`)

- **15 files + 2 subdirs** deleted from `.scratch/` (Bucket A — shipped/superseded/regenerable)
- **3 untracked top-level cleanup**: `todo.md`, `threads/`, `records/` (auto-generated click extracts)
- **2 `__pycache__` dirs** under X010/X011 deleted, with `__pycache__/` + `*.pyc` added to `.gitignore`
- **coco-log** entry appended

Net: 21 files changed, ~2189 lines deleted.

## One thing to flag

Between my pre-commit ls and post-commit ls, **four files vanished from `.scratch/` that I didn't touch**:

- `handoff-distiller-cost.md` (Bucket C)
- `handoff-lab-phase-2c.md` (Bucket C)
- `p3-handoff-rig1-t52.md` (Bucket B)
- `reckoner/strategy.md` (Bucket B — and shows as `D` in git since it was tracked)

Looks like parallel cleanup activity (possibly you in another window — there's also an untracked `experiments/X017-test-redundancy/artifacts/2026-05-02-framework-core.yaml` that wasn't there at session start, so something is touching this tree). I left them alone.

The `reckoner/strategy.md` deletion is sitting unstaged. Want me to stage it (and remove the now-empty `reckoner/` dir) as a follow-up, or leave it for whatever's doing the parallel work?

## Final `.scratch/` state

```
attachments-spec-parked.md           ← Bucket B, kept
cartograph-design-synthesis.md       ← Bucket B, kept
clockworks-retrofit-candidates.md    ← Bucket B, kept

observation-triage/                  ← Bucket B, kept
sonnet-eval/                         ← Bucket C, active
vision-planner-quality.md            ← Bucket B, kept
```

Down from 33 top-level entries to 12. Want me to revisit the Bucket B archive question now that the tree's smaller, or leave it?

## Memos

# Handoff — Test Trim Workflow

**From session:** `88a35e7d-5e4d-4685-96a1-68607fc9c7f8` (free-2), 2026-05-02.

## TL;DR

The infrastructure for trimming tests is built and committed across three
repos. You can pick up next session by reading
**`/workspace/nexus/docs/guides/trimming-tests.md`** and starting on a
package — `framework/core` is the recommended first real trim (small,
known to have ~74% pure-redundant candidates).

## What landed

### `/workspace/nexus`

| Commit | What |
|---|---|
| `56914fb` | Per-package `test:coverage` script + monorepo aggregator. `pnpm coverage` runs all 24 packages with V8 instrumentation, merges per-package lcovs, prints summary table. Standard exclude list (`**/*.test.{ts,js}`, `**/*.d.ts`, `**/test-helpers.ts`, `**/*-test-fixture.ts`, `**/conformance/**`, `coverage/**`). |
| `b192d84` | Threshold gate in `scripts/coverage-report.ts` (floors 67% line / 80% branch / 53% function — round numbers with ~1pp headroom over baseline 67.96/80.05/53.05). `--no-check` flag opts out. Tail-bias truncation in `spider/engines/review.ts` so failure verdicts survive 4KB capture. CI swapped `pnpm test` → `pnpm coverage`. |
| `1508818` | `scripts/test-uniqueness.ts` per-test attribution analyzer + `docs/guides/trimming-tests.md` workflow guide. (Originally used FNDA+BRDA — see next commit.) |
| `1122e8b` | Methodology correction: line coverage (DA records) is in fact reliable for per-test attribution. Earlier commit message in `1508818` was wrong about this; corrected here with raw V8 profile evidence. Switched analyzer to DA records. Workflow guide updated to remove the false claim and add a "Note on the lcov import baseline" explaining the 62-of-124-lines-hit-on-import phenomenon that originally tripped me up. |

### `/workspace/vibers`

| Commit | What |
|---|---|
| `cedc5f1` | `spider.testCommand` and `spider.variables.testCommand` changed `pnpm test` → `pnpm coverage` so coverage threshold failures flow into the spider review engine's mechanical-check step → reviewer prompt → revise engine. |

### `/workspace/nexus-mk2`

| Commit | What |
|---|---|
| `112b9589` | coco-log: framework coverage scaffolding baseline. |
| `b3b0a3ea` | coco-log: coverage threshold gate + tail-bias truncation. |
| `6b94d13e` | coco-log: per-test uniqueness analyzer. |
| (this session, end) | X017 spec + experiment-index update + this handoff (next commit). |

## How to run a trim

From `/workspace/nexus`:

```sh
# 1. Pick a package
pnpm test:uniqueness <pkg>            # e.g. framework/core, plugins/spider, plugins/clerk
                                      # accepts shortforms — see docs/guides/trimming-tests.md
# Optional: pnpm test:uniqueness <pkg> --workers 4 --filter <regex>

# 2. Read the report
cat coverage/uniqueness/<pkg-flat-name>.md

# 3. Spot-check redundancy candidates → decide keep/delete using the
#    assertion peek column. Watch for parameter sweeps that share
#    line coverage but assert distinct input→output mappings.

# 4. Edit test files to delete decided candidates. No auto-delete.

# 5. Verify
cd packages/<pkg> && pnpm test:coverage   # tests still pass
cd /workspace/nexus && pnpm coverage      # aggregate floor still holds (exit 0)

# 6. Re-run analyzer to confirm
pnpm test:uniqueness <pkg>                # cached, fast

# 7. Commit per package, update the per-package status table at
#    the bottom of docs/guides/trimming-tests.md.
```

If `pnpm coverage` exits 1, you over-trimmed; restore some tests
and try again. The aggregate gate is the safety net.

## Recommended order of attack

(Also documented in `docs/guides/trimming-tests.md`.)

1. **`framework/core`** — 39 tests / 311 test lines. Probed: 29
   pure-redundant (74%). Smallest real package; validates the
   workflow on a real trim.
2. **`framework/arbor`** — 113 tests / 1,833 test lines. Probed:
   90 pure-redundant (80%). Small monolith, two files.
3. **`plugins/stacks`** — already 97.7% line coverage. Likely few
   wins; do it for completeness.
4. Mid-size plugins (`tools`, `codexes`, `copilot`, `lattice`,
   `lattice-discord`, `fabricator`, `loom`).
5. Larger plugins (`oculus`, `parlour`, `cartograph`, `claude-code`,
   `sentinel`, `astrolabe`, `clockworks`, `reckoner`).
6. **The headline targets:** `clerk` (7,148 test lines), `clockworks`
   if not yet done, **`spider`** (18,714 test lines — the headline
   prize). Spider is ~6× larger than the next biggest, with
   modest 70.7% line coverage; expected biggest absolute win.

## What to capture for X017

Each trim session is a data point for **`X017 — Test Redundancy in
Agent-Written Code`** (spec at
`/workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X017-test-redundancy/spec.md`).

Per package, append a YAML row to
`/workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X017-test-redundancy/artifacts/<YYYY-MM-DD>-<pkg>.yaml`
with:

- pre/post counts (test files, test lines, total tests, pure-redundant)
- pre/post coverage (line / branch / function from `pnpm coverage`)
- candidates reviewed / deleted / kept
- a sampling of "kept" tests with reason categories (parameter sweep /
  edge case / error path / representative / other)
- wall-clock duration
- commit SHA

The spec lists five hypotheses (H1–H5); the YAML rows are the data
that lets you eventually evaluate them.

If you'd rather not collect X017 data on a given session — totally
fine. The trim still works as straight engineering. The experiment is
opportunistic; data points are valuable but not required for trim
itself.

## Things I almost got wrong (so you don't)

These are the gotchas I burned hours on. They're documented in code
comments and the workflow guide, but flagging here too:

1. **`--test-name-pattern` separator is a SINGLE SPACE.** Not `' > '`,
   not `/`, not `>`. node:test joins suite titles + test name with one
   space. Wrong separator silently matches zero tests but **still
   reports `count=1`** because the file itself is wrapped as a
   passing meta-test. So you can't tell from the test count alone
   whether the pattern actually worked. Always check FNDA/BRDA values
   in the lcov to verify a real test ran.

2. **The lcov import baseline is real, not a bug.** When you run a
   test in isolation, ~half the lines of any imported source file
   show hit=1 in the lcov *before* the test body runs. Those are
   genuinely covered at module-load time (top-level imports,
   constants, function declarations). It's NOT "every line marked
   hit=1 regardless of execution" — that's what I initially thought,
   and I was wrong. Verify hit-count distribution with
   `awk -F',' '{print $2}' | sort | uniq -c`.

3. **Tests with name collisions get skipped.** If two tests in a file
   share the same `<suite> <name>` joined string, `--test-name-pattern`
   can't uniquely select either. The analyzer warns and excludes
   them rather than misattribute. Real but rare.

## Open questions / further work

- **Branch-coverage signal.** Current analyzer uses line coverage. The
  guide notes that two tests covering the same line but taking
  different branches will both appear redundant under this signal.
  The aggregate gate's branch floor (80%) catches over-trimming, but
  if you want a finer signal, the FNDA+BRDA implementation is in git
  at `1508818`. Keep DA for now (consistent with the gate, easier to
  inspect); revisit if data suggests otherwise.

- **Per-test wall-clock is ~2× the package's `pnpm test` time** on
  first run, ~0.5s on cached re-runs. For `spider` (762 tests) expect
  roughly 4–5 minutes fresh. Worth letting it run while you do other
  things.

- **Greedy reduction is non-optimal.** Picks one valid redundant set,
  not the maximum. If we ever want tighter trim, run with `--filter`
  scopes (file-by-file) and compare. Out of scope for the first pass.

- **Empty describe blocks left after deletion.** The analyzer flags
  but doesn't auto-clean. If you delete every test in a describe, the
  describe shell stays behind. Worth a sweep at the end of each
  package's trim.

## Quick-start commands

```sh
# Get oriented
cat /workspace/nexus/docs/guides/trimming-tests.md

# Read the spec for X017 (if you want to instrument)
cat /workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X017-test-redundancy/spec.md

# Start a trim
cd /workspace/nexus
pnpm test:uniqueness framework/core
cat coverage/uniqueness/framework-core.md
```

## Files I'd read next session, in order

1. This file (you are here).
2. `/workspace/nexus/docs/guides/trimming-tests.md` — the workflow.
3. `/workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X017-test-redundancy/spec.md` —
   the experiment frame.
4. `/workspace/nexus/scripts/test-uniqueness.ts` — only if you need
   to debug something or extend the analyzer. Otherwise the doc is
   enough.

That's it. Have fun cutting.

# Implement-Engine Sonnet → Opus Switch Investigation

## Verdict

**No commit, click, planning doc, or session note explains the switch.** It was a side-channel manual edit by Sean to `/workspace/vibers/guild.json` — undocumented anywhere in the project record.

## Mechanism

The implement-engine model resolves from a single guild-config field, not from any per-engine setting:

`/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/animator/src/animator.ts:167-171`
```ts
function resolveModel(): string {
  const g = guild();
  const guildConfig = g.guildConfig();
  return guildConfig.settings?.model ?? 'sonnet';
}
```

So the implement-engine model is whatever `settings.model` is in `/workspace/vibers/guild.json` at session-launch time. Default is `'sonnet'`.

## Cutover window — narrowed to ~17.5 hours

From `.scratch/sonnet-eval/sessions.jsonl`, decoded by base36 session-ID timestamp:

- **Last Sonnet implement session:** `ses-mnqkkziu-659b7c51` (writ `w-mnqkkxpw-b6c1fccc6796`, "Astrolabe MVP Part 2") — **2026-04-08T21:38:15.894Z**
- **First Opus implement session:** `ses-mnrhjd9s-87caf67b` (writ `w-mnrhjait-e247f911040b`, "[Repost] Oculus - Spider Page: Clickable title") — **2026-04-09T13:00:47.728Z**

Cross-checked via Coco's own transcripts reading `/workspace/vibers/guild.json`:

- `2026-04-07T19:36:56Z` (transcript `09eba43a…`, line 306) — file showed **`"model": "sonnet"`**
- `2026-04-09T16:42:14Z` (transcript `a11b80ab…`, line 67) — file showed **`"model": "opus"`**

The flip happened between **2026-04-07T19:37Z and 2026-04-09T13:00Z**.

## Why no commit was found

Vibers became a git repo on **2026-04-10T14:56:00Z** (`ecc2509a` "initial commit: guild state snapshot" by Coco). The very first commit already had `"model": "opus"` baked in. There is no prior history of `guild.json` to surface the change from.

I scanned every Coco-driven `Edit`/`Write`/`Bash` against `/workspace/vibers/guild.json` in the cutover window across all 149 transcripts. The only edits in that window were unrelated: adding `codexes.settings.draftRoot` (2026-04-07T19:37, transcript `09eba43a…` line 310) and earlier spider variable + rigTemplate cleanup. **None of Coco's tool calls changed the `settings.model` field.** Therefore Sean edited `guild.json` directly outside of any Coco session.

Framework side: `/workspace/nexus/` has no commit in the window that altered `resolveModel()` or its consumers (`git log -S 'settings?.model'` shows the field has been read this way since `372be6f`, 2026-04-07).

## Why no click or doc explains it

- No click in vibers or sanctum mentions changing the implementer model. The only related click is `c-mokdz3sr-7ec0d375113b` (created 2026-04-29T18:26Z under cost-umbrella `c-mok4nke6`), which proposes switching the implementer **back to Sonnet** as the P0 cost lever — and frames Opus as the established default. Still live.
- No session note from 2026-04-07/08/09 mentions changing the implementer model. The closest adjacent context is X015's spec at `experiments/X015-spec-detail-model-substitute/spec.md` (filed 2026-04-06, commit `3ef440e`), which asked the *opposite* question: can spec detail let Sonnet match Opus? X015 was filed as Draft and never activated.
- The 2026-04-25 cost case study (`docs/case-studies/2026-04-25-implement-cost-investigation.md`) and its supporting record `c-modxx30v` blame the Apr-16/17 cost step-change on the task-manifest commit `920e65ca` and click-inlining `260f5cf9` — they don't reference the earlier Apr-9 model swap at all.

## Inference about motivation

Circumstantial only — Sean did not write down a reason. The Apr 7-8 Sonnet sessions ran several substantive commissions (Astrolabe MVP parts 1 and 2, Spider template interpolation). By the 2026-04-14T175533 session note Sean was actively debugging Opus-specific parallel-tool-call behavior, indicating he was committed to Opus for non-trivial work by then. The Apr-29 cost-optimization landscape (`.scratch/cost-optimization-landscape.md`, click `c-mok4nke6`) treats Opus as the entrenched default and Sonnet as a future-savings option not yet pulled.

## What was unclear / unresolvable

- Whether the switch was a deliberate quality call after an Apr-8 Sonnet failure, a cost-experiment ramp-up for Astrolabe MVP, or just Sean reaching for the better model and not bothering to log it. Without a commit, click, or note, this is judgment-call territory and the patron is the only source.

content.text:
Honest answer — I don't think it's earning its keep.

**What the log claims to be:** a research instrument for understanding patron-side work routing. The `commissionable` flag was supposed to capture "could this have been an autonomous commission?" so we could analyze which work routes through Coco-direct vs. the workforce.

**What the data actually shows** (309 entries):
- 282 non-commissionable, 27 commissionable (~91% / 9%)
- Of the non-commissionable: 280 tagged `sanctum`, 1 `interactive`, rest `sanctum`
- The valid-justification set narrowed from `{interactive, sanctum, decisional}` to just `{sanctum}` in the CLAUDE.md

**The canary:** when a classification taxonomy collapses to one valid bin, it's not classifying anything. Every entry says "I did sanctum work I did myself" — which is tautological for Coco.

**What it duplicates:**
- `session`, `date`, commit subject — all in `git log` already (Session trailer + author = Coco)
- The "tight cluster of commits → one entry" rule is the only thing that adds curation, but it's a one-line restatement of what could be reconstructed from commit ranges

**What's left of original value:**
- The 27 `commissionable: true` entries are the only data points that carry signal beyond git log — "Coco did work the workforce could have done." But there's no annotation on those, so we can't analyze *why* they were Coco-direct. The bare count (~9%) is the only fact, and it's not actionable.

**My read:** this started as a real instrument, the taxonomy didn't survive contact with reality (turns out almost everything Coco does is meta/sanctum work, by definition of Coco's role), and now it's a per-commit ritual that costs a tool call and a stage step every commit and produces a near-constant.

**Options:**

1. **Retire it.** Drop the section from CLAUDE.md, leave the file in place as a historical artifact. Recover the per-commit overhead. `git log --author=Coco` becomes the source of truth.
2. **Sharpen it.** Re-introduce a non-degenerate taxonomy that actually splits the work — e.g. distinguish "design conversation," "system maintenance," "experiment infrastructure," "doc curation." Then the data answers something.
3. **Narrow it.** Only log `commissionable: true` entries — the genuinely interesting cases where Coco did work the workforce could have. ~10× less volume, more signal density.

I'd lean (1) unless you have a question in mind that the log is supposed to answer. (3) is a defensible middle path. (2) is real work that I'd only do if there's a concrete hypothesis to support.

What's the question you'd want it to answer?

content: You asked why we maintain the coco-log; I argued it's drifted into ceremony since the justification taxonomy collapsed to a single bin and the data duplicates git log. Next: you decide retire, sharpen, or narrow. (disable recaps in /config)

delete legacy quality-review scripts

    Old scripts fully superseded by instrument-review.sh.

    Session: 905f54f8-fd5c-49df-9294-b856202a7740

## Coco Log

Coco maintains `experiments/data/coco-log.yaml` — a running log of every unit of work handled directly rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission. This is a standing research instrument tracking the patron-side work stream.

**When to log:** Every commit you make gets a coco-log entry. Bundle the log update into the same commit as the work.

**What counts as one entry:** One commit, or a tight cluster of commits toward a single goal. If you make 3 commits that are all part of "build the integration scorer," that's one entry — log it on the last commit of the cluster.

**Schema fields:**
- `session` — your Claude session ID
- `date` — today's date (YYYY-MM-DD)
- `item` — one-line description of the work
- `commissionable` — boolean: could this work have been an autonomous commission?
- `justification` — required when `commissionable: false`. Pick from: 
  - `sanctum`: any changes to /workspace/nexus-mk2 should use this
  - **NO OTHER JUSTIFICATIONS ARE ALLOWED**

Commits are recoverable from the git log via the Session trailer — no need to record SHAs in the log.

**The commissionable judgment:**
- `false` — Work that *can't* be commissioned: interactive/decisional work requiring real-time iteration with Sean, sanctum/experiment/meta work, or work whose output is a decision rather than an artifact.
- `true` — Work that *could* have been a commission but wasn't. No further annotation needed — the bare fact is the data point. Session logs and commit history provide the context if the "why" matters later.

Pick from the justification list honestly. Having to choose from a constrained set keeps the classification rigorous — you can't rationalize everything as "interactive" when it was really just convenient.

## Commit Process

When making a git commit:

1. **Use Coco's git identity** via environment variables (see Git Identity section).
2. **Include the Session trailer** with your Claude session ID.
3. **Append a coco-log entry** to `experiments/data/coco-log.yaml` describing the work. Stage the log update alongside the work files so they land in the same commit.
4. **Commit format:**

       GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local \
       git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
       <commit message>

       Session: <session-id>
       EOF
       )"

If multiple commits form a single logical unit of work, add the coco-log entry on the last commit of the cluster.

## Output

When presenting plans or options to Sean, use this general structure:

1. **Context** — Brief summary of the current state

content.content:
# Nexus Mk 2.1 — Sanctum

This is the sanctum: the patron-side home base for the Nexus Mk 2.1 project. It holds experiments, research, operational tooling, and the domain model — but not the framework source code. The framework lives at `/workspace/nexus/`.

Nexus Mk 2.1 is also a documented experiment — Sean is exploring AI-enabled development practices and intends to publish findings.

## Project Structure

```
nexus-mk2/  (the sanctum)
├── experiments/                 # Research & experiment tracking
│   ├── X001–X012/              #   Numbered experiments, each with spec.md + artifacts/
│   └── ethnography/            #   Qualitative research on human-agent interaction
│       ├── interviews/          #   Ethnographer interview transcripts (timestamped)
│       ├── session-notes/       #   Per-session observations (new/ → reviewed/ pipeline)
│       └── transcripts/         #   Design session transcripts, interview source material
├── domain/                      # Separate git repo, mounted read-only for agents
│   ├── ontology/                #   TypeScript type definitions — formal domain model
│   ├── requirements/            #   System requirements (YAML)
│   └── backlog/                 #   Feature ideas not yet commissioned
├── docs/                        # Sanctum-side documentation
│   ├── future/                  #   Backlog: known-gaps, uncommissioned feature ideas
│   └── archive/                 #   Completed commissions, deprecated docs
├── bin/                         # Shell scripts for operations
│   ├── coco.sh                  #   Launch Coco session
│   └── ...                      #   Operational scripts (commissions go through `nsg commission-post`)
├── .claude/                     # Claude Code agent configuration
│   ├── CLAUDE.md                #   THIS FILE — shared instructions for all agents
│   ├── agents/                  #   Agent-specific instructions (coco.md, ethnographer.md)
│   ├── skills/                  #   Reusable skill definitions (wrap-up/)
│   └── worktrees/               #   Snapshot of old worktree state (historical)
├── .artifacts/                  # Build & assessment artifacts (JSON, timestamped)
├── .scratch/                    # Ephemeral working space
│   ├── recent-sessions/         #   Coco session summaries (read at startup for continuity)
│   └── todo/                    #   Tabled items: parked specs/analysis to pick up later
└── .locks/                      # Feature locks for concurrent agent coordination
```

## Related Repositories

- **`/workspace/nexus/`** — the framework source code (TypeScript packages, published to npm)
- **`/workspace/vibers/`** — the live guild workspace where animas operate

## Key Concepts

- **Guild** — an instantiated workspace where animas operate. Created via `nsg init`.
- **Anima** — an AI identity with a name, role, curriculum, and temperament.
- **Commission** — a posted unit of work from the patron. Creates a `mandate` writ.
- **Writ** — a typed, tree-structured record kept in the guild's books with a lifecycle (`new → open → stuck → completed/failed/cancelled`). The type names the kind of record: `mandate` for an obligation. Dispatch to a rig is opt-in per writ type.
- **Click** — an atomic decision-node managed by the Ratchet apparatus. Tracks questions and inquiries (the reasoning domain), organized in a tree. Lifecycle: `live → parked → concluded/dropped`. Clicks are the session-continuity mechanism for interactive work; writs track obligations.
- **Clockworks** — the event-driven dispatch layer. Standing orders bind event patterns to handlers (engines or summons).
- **Session** — a single agent invocation through the session funnel (manifest → launch → record).
- **Conversation** — multi-turn interaction grouping multiple sessions via `--resume`.

## Foundational Documents

- **[Project Philosophy](/workspace/nexus/docs/philosophy.md)** — the "why" of this project: experiment goals, Mk 2.0 vs 2.1 differences, the human-as-user model.
- **[Guild Metaphor](/workspace/nexus/docs/guild-metaphor.md)** — the conceptual model and vocabulary for the system. Read this to understand guild terminology (anima, commission, writ, clockworks, etc.) and write in the correct register.

## Directives

- **Self-document for other agents.** Write commit messages, code comments, and documentation with the assumption that your primary audience is other agents who will continue the work. Be precise and concise; include enough context for an agent to pick up where you left off.
- **Respect the human boundary.** Certain agents (like Coco) are interactive and have their own interaction rules. All other agents are autonomous and should minimize unnecessary human interaction.
- **Commit early and often.** Make small, atomic commits as work is completed. Do not accumulate large uncommitted changesets. Never leave uncommitted or untracked files in the project root. This is critical in a multi-agent environment where conflicts are a real risk.
- **Minimize conflict surface.** Structure work to reduce the likelihood of git conflicts with other agents. Prefer adding new files over modifying shared ones. When modifying shared files, keep changes narrow and well-scoped. Commit and merge promptly rather than holding long-lived branches.
- **Date-prefix experiment artifacts.** Files in `experiments/X*/artifacts/` use the naming convention `YYYY-MM-DD-<slug>.ext` (e.g., `2026-04-03-findings.md`). Use the date the artifact was created. This keeps artifacts chronologically sortable within each experiment.

## Coco Log

Coco maintains `experiments/data/coco-log.yaml` — a running log of every unit of work handled directly rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission. This is a standing research instrument tracking the patron-side work stream.

**When to log:** Every commit you make gets a coco-log entry. Bundle the log update into the same commit as the work.

**What counts as one entry:** One commit, or a tight cluster of commits toward a single goal. If you make 3 commits that are all part of "build the integration scorer," that's one entry — log it on the last commit of the cluster.

**Schema fields:**
- `session` — your Claude session ID
- `date` — today's date (YYYY-MM-DD)
- `item` — one-line description of the work
- `commissionable` — boolean: could this work have been an autonomous commission?
- `justification` — required when `commissionable: false`. Pick from: 
  - `sanctum`: any changes to /workspace/nexus-mk2 should use this
  - **NO OTHER JUSTIFICATIONS ARE ALLOWED**

Commits are recoverable from the git log via the Session trailer — no need to record SHAs in the log.

**The commissionable judgment:**
- `false` — Work that *can't* be commissioned: interactive/decisional work requiring real-time iteration with Sean, sanctum/experiment/meta work, or work whose output is a decision rather than an artifact.
- `true` — Work that *could* have been a commission but wasn't. No further annotation needed — the bare fact is the data point. Session logs and commit history provide the context if the "why" matters later.

Pick from the justification list honestly. Having to choose from a constrained set keeps the classification rigorous — you can't rationalize everything as "interactive" when it was really just convenient.

## Commit Process

When making a git commit:

1. **Use Coco's git identity** via environment variables (see Git Identity section).
2. **Include the Session trailer** with your Claude session ID.
3. **Append a coco-log entry** to `experiments/data/coco-log.yaml` describing the work. Stage the log update alongside the work files so they land in the same commit.
4. **Commit format:**

       GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local \
       git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
       <commit message>

       Session: <session-id>
       EOF
       )"

If multiple commits form a single logical unit of work, add the coco-log entry on the last commit of the cluster.

# This was a standing research instrument: one entry per Coco commit,

Base directory for this skill: /workspace/nexus-mk2/.claude/skills/wrap-up

# Wrap-Up: Complete Work and Generate Session Summary

## Generate Summary

Generate a structured summary of the current session. This summary serves one audience:

**The Scribe** — as input for session notes and published material.

Session-to-session continuity is now carried by **clicks** (see Coco's agent file and the clicks skill). Before generating the wrap-up summary, conclude or park any clicks touched this session — the click tree is what the next Coco session will read for orientation, not this wrap-up file.

<expected-format>
# Session Summary

## What we did

- Bulleted list of concrete actions taken (files created/modified, decisions made, commits pushed). Reference specific files and commit hashes.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- What was decided, including explicit "we chose NOT to" decisions. Brief rationale for each.

## Next steps & open questions

- Concrete actions for the next session or for agents to pick up.
- Open questions which were raised but not answered

### Notable moments

Capture quotes, insights, or exchanges that stand out — either for system design or for the research/experiment narrative.

For each notable moment:
- Include **direct quotes from Sean** when available
- Flag **why it's notable** (e.g., "shifted the project's core abstraction", "surfaced a tension between X and Y", "revealed an assumption about how agents should work")
- Prioritize moments where the human changed direction, challenged an assumption, or articulated something new about the project's purpose

### Experiment observations

Flag anything relevant to active experiments. Omit this section if nothing applies.

<expected-format>

## Complete Work

After wrapping up, **ALWAYS**:

- Commit any changes you have made, if any
- Push main to the remote

## Reminders

Check for things Sean should be nudged about:

- **Ethnographer interview:** Check the most recent interview file in `experiments/ethnography/interviews/` (files named `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md`). If the most recent one is more than a day old — or if none exist yet — remind Sean to run an ethnographer session. Keep it light: *"It's been a couple days since your last ethnographer interview — might be a good time to check in."*

## Guidelines

- Keep the whole summary under ~500 words. Brevity is a feature.
- Do not editorialize beyond the "why it's notable" flags.
- Write the summary to `experiments/ethnography/session-notes/new/<timestamp>.md`. where `<timestamp>` is the current date and time formatted as `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md` (use the actual current time — don't round to the nearest hour). Each session gets its own file — never overwrite a previous one. This file is for the ethnographer and must NOT be auto-deleted — it accumulates until the ethnographer reviews it.
- Commit all `session-notes`, and `experiments/data/transcripts/` changes to git and push those changes. The transcripts directory may contain files from concurrent sessions — commit whatever is there.
- In addition to writing the summary, share it in the chat for review and inclusion in session transcripts

input.content:
# Session Summary

**Session:** `fab0eacc-1668-404d-a179-8bff1285fa08`
**Date:** 2026-05-02

## What we did

- **`.scratch/` triage** (continued from pre-compaction). Started with 33 items.
  - Bucket A delete pass + top-level cruft cleanup (todo.md, threads/, records/, X010/X011 `__pycache__`); added Python `.gitignore` entries → commit `bcc4a1c7`.
  - Bucket B archive pass: moved 5 design docs (attachments-spec-parked, clockworks-retrofit-candidates, laboratory-framework-recon, low-conf-confirms, vision-planner-quality) to `docs/archive/design-sessions/`; staged Sean's parallel deletions of cartograph-design-synthesis, observation-triage, reckoner/strategy → commit `67fdf277`.
  - Final `.scratch/` state: 4 entries (cost-optimization-landscape, handoff-test-trim, sonnet-eval/, this session's notes).
- **Retired the coco-log** (`experiments/data/coco-log.yaml`). Sean asked why we maintain it; analysis showed ~91% `commissionable: false` / `justification: sanctum`, with the valid-justification set narrowed to a single bin. Recommended retirement (option 1 of 3 presented). Sean concurred. Dropped the section from `coco.md` and the per-commit log step from Commit Process; rewrote the YAML header as RETIRED, preserved the file as historical artifact → commit `985da02b`.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Coco-log retired** — `git log --author=Coco` + Session trailer is sufficient; the classification taxonomy had collapsed to a single bin and was no longer classifying anything. File preserved as historical artifact (NOT deleted, NOT migrated).
- **`cost-optimization-landscape.md` kept in `.scratch/`** per Sean's explicit exception during Bucket B.
- **Chose NOT to** introduce a sharpened taxonomy or narrow-the-log middle path — Sean preferred clean retirement over reform.

## Next steps & open questions

- Optional follow-ups still parked (not actioned): phase 2c design conversation, probe-git-range fix verification (`20b857ee` may resolve a live click), spider 0.1.292 ordering bomb fix.
- Test trim workflow remains ready to pick up (`/workspace/nexus-mk2/.scratch/handoff-test-trim.md` → recommended start: `framework/core`).

### Notable moments

- **Sean: *"why are we maintaining a 'coco-commit' log?"*** — A bare, direct probing question. Notable because it surfaced an instrument that had drifted from research tool into per-commit ceremony. The pattern is worth naming: when a classification system's valid answer set converges to one option (here `justification: sanctum`), it has stopped classifying. This is a generalizable smell — not specific to logs. Any taxonomy that always returns the same answer is dead bookkeeping. Sean's instinct to challenge ongoing instruments is doing real work in keeping the project lean.
- **The retirement itself was clean** — three options presented (retire / sharpen / narrow), Sean picked retire in two words ("yeah, let's retire it"), executed in one commit. The decisional pattern: when there's no live hypothesis the instrument is supposed to test, retire it; don't reform.

### Experiment observations

None — no active experiment work this session.

- **The retirement itself was clean** — three options presented (retire / sharpen / narrow), Sean picked retire in two words ("yeah, let's retire it"), executed in one commit. The decisional pattern: when there's no live hypothesis the instrument is supposed to test, retire it; don't reform.

### Experiment observations

None — no active experiment work this session.

Wrapped and pushed (`31f1ce2d`). Session summary below for the record.

---

# Session Summary

**Session:** `fab0eacc-1668-404d-a179-8bff1285fa08`
**Date:** 2026-05-02

## What we did

- **`.scratch/` triage** (continued from pre-compaction). Started with 33 items.
  - Bucket A delete + top-level cruft + Python `.gitignore` → `bcc4a1c7`.
  - Bucket B archive: 5 design docs moved to `docs/archive/design-sessions/`; staged Sean's parallel deletions → `67fdf277`.
  - Final `.scratch/`: 4 entries.
- **Retired the coco-log.** Sean asked why we maintain it; analysis showed ~91% `commissionable: false` / `justification: sanctum`, with the valid-justification set narrowed to one bin. Dropped the section from `coco.md` and the Commit Process step; rewrote the YAML header as RETIRED, preserved the file → `985da02b`.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Coco-log retired** — `git log --author=Coco` + Session trailer is sufficient. File preserved as historical artifact.
- **`cost-optimization-landscape.md` kept in `.scratch/`** per Sean's exception.
- **Chose NOT to** sharpen or narrow the log — clean retirement preferred over reform.

## Next steps & open questions

- Parked: phase 2c design, probe-git-range click verification, spider 0.1.292 ordering fix.
- Test trim workflow ready to start (handoff in `.scratch/handoff-test-trim.md`).

### Notable moments

- **Sean: *"why are we maintaining a 'coco-commit' log?"*** — Bare, direct probe. Surfaced an instrument that had drifted from research tool into per-commit ceremony. Generalizable smell: when a classification's valid-answer set converges to one option, it has stopped classifying. Sean's instinct to challenge ongoing instruments keeps the project lean.
- **Clean retirement pattern** — three options presented (retire / sharpen / narrow), Sean picked in two words, executed in one commit. When there's no live hypothesis to test, retire; don't reform.

### Experiment observations

None — no active experiment work this session.
